chapters.summary: Council staff announces the hearing of the Committee to Combat Hate, instructs attendees on testimony submission, and reminds everyone to silence electronic devices and not approach the dais.
chapters.title: Council staff welcomes attendees and reviews hearing procedures

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to today's New York City Council hearing for the Committee to Combat Hate.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you would like to testify, you must fill out a testimony slip with one of the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You can also submit testimony at testimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all electronic devices, and no one may approach a dais at any time during this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Yusef Salaam introduces himself as chair of the Committee to Combat Hate, thanks attendees, and notes that today's oversight hearing examines the role of community engagement in preventing and responding to hate and bias.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam welcomes attendees and introduces hearing topic

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: My name is Yusef-Salaam and I serve as the chair to the Committee to Combat Hate.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I would like to first start off by thanking everyone for joining us here today.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And I would also like to recognize my colleagues as they come into the room.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: None yet, but...

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam discusses how NYC's diversity is its greatest strength and how hate crimes seek to divide communities, targeting not just individuals but entire faith, race, gender identity, and background groups, with harm that reverberates across generations.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam: NYC diversity as strength and hate crimes' impact

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Today's oversight hearing examines the role of community engagement in preventing and responding to hate and bias.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: New York City is one of the most diverse places on earth.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: That diversity is our greatest strength, and it is a source of great pride and unity for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Hate crimes strike at the very heart of this strength by seeking to divide, seeking to pit us against each other, as opposed to uniting one another.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: They target not just the individual, but all our communities, sending a message of fear and exclusion to all who share the victim's faith, the victim's race,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: the victim's gender identity, or the victim's background.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: That harm is immediate.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: But it can reverberate throughout generations.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam reports that NYC recorded a 20% citywide reduction in hate crime complaints in 2025, with decreases across all four major bias categories: religion, race/ethnicity, gender/sexual orientation, and age. He attributes progress to sustained investments in prevention and community outreach.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam: 2025 hate crime trends show 20% citywide reduction

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: At our last hearing, we examined some very concerning multi-year trends of increasing hate crime and bias.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Yet in 2025, we also saw some encouraging trends.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Last year, New York City recorded a 20% citywide reduction in hate crime complaints across the board compared to the prior year.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: This includes decreases across all four major categories of bias, religion, race and ethnicity, gender and sexual orientation, and of course, age.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Much of this progress reflects sustained investments in prevention, in youth leadership, in community outreach, and in coordinated citywide action.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: We are here to learn more about this work and how it can be supported and expanded in the coming years.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam notes that despite overall progress, Buddhist communities saw dramatic increases in reported hate crimes and anti-female crimes rose as well. He emphasizes that aggregate data can obscure real challenges and that no community should be left behind.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam: some communities still face spikes in hate crimes

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And at the same time, we must also take an honest look at where the fight against hate is falling short.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Even in a year of overall progress, some communities still suffered spikes in hate-biased violence.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Buddhist communities, for example, saw a dramatic increase in reported hate crimes and anti-female crimes rose as well.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: These variations remind us that aggregate data can obscure real challenges and harms.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: We must ensure that no community is left behind and that no community is ignored.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam discusses how hate crimes are chronically underreported as many turn to community organizations rather than government. He introduces the three testifying offices: OPHC, the Mayor's Office to Combat Antisemitism, and the Mayor's Office of Faith-Based and Community Partnerships.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam: underreporting and importance of community partnerships

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: We also know that hate crimes are chronically underreported, not necessarily because of distrust in government, although that can be a part of it.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Instead, many turn to their communities for trusted support when they have been the target of hate and bias.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: This is why it's crucial that we partner with community-based organizations to build trust, to encourage reporting, and to connect affected individuals and communities with the resources and support they need.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Today we will hear from three offices whose work reflects this principle.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: The Office for the Prevention of Hate Crimes,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: the Mayor's Office to Combat Antisemitism, and the Mayor's Office of Faith-Based and Community Partnerships.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I look forward to a thoughtful discussion surrounding these issues, as well as feedback and testimony from the administration and, of course, members of our public.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel administers the oath to the administration panel, who swear to tell the truth before the committee.
chapters.title: Administration of oath to testifying panel

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I would now like to pass it to our committee council to administer the oaths.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you could raise your right hand, please, and repeat after me.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I swear to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Vijay Ramjatin, Executive Director of OPHC, thanks Mayor Mamdani and Deputy Mayor Francois for elevating hate prevention as central to public safety. He describes OPHC's move to the Office of Community Safety as a clear statement that preventing hate is not peripheral to public safety.
chapters.title: OPHC Director Ramjatin: Mamdani administration's commitment to hate prevention

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: There it is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Salam, members of the Committee to Combat Hate, and all members of the New York City Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: My name is Vijay Ramjatin, and I am the Executive Director for the New York City Office for the Prevention of Hate Crimes, also known as OPHC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Which under Mayor Mamdani's leadership is now proudly part of the city's new office for community safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Before I begin, I want to thank Mayor Mamdani, the Deputy Mayor of Community Safety, Renee Francois, and the Commissioner of the Office of Community Safety, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Ayesha Delaney-Bramsey, for their bold vision.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And for making the prevention of hate a genuine priority of this administration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The decision to bring OPSC into the Office of Community Safety was not a routine reorganization.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: It was a clear statement of value.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But preventing hate before it ever takes hold is not peripheral to public safety, but rather it is central to it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That commitment has finally given offices like ours the mandate, the partners, and the support to do the work at the scale our city deserves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: For too long, hate prevention has often been treated as an afterthought, expected to solve growing challenges without the resources necessary to meet the moment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This administration has made a different choice.

chapters.summary: Ramjatin details the Mamdani administration's unprecedented $26 million funding increase to OPHC, an 800% increase, calling it an investment worthy of the most diverse city in the world. He says the funding will expand programming, deepen partnerships, hire staff, and build a city prevention infrastructure.
chapters.title: OPHC Director Ramjatin: historic $26M funding increase of 800%

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The Mamdani administration has made an unprecedented commitment to hate prevention by increasing funding to OPHC by $26 million, an increase of more than 800%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This historic investment recognizes that preventing hate is not simply about responding after

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: incidents occur.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: It is about building resilient communities where hate cannot take root in the first place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: For the first time, OPHC is being resourced at a scale that matches both the urgency of its mission and the diversity of the city it serves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That investment will allow us to expand programming

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: deepen partnerships, reach more communities, hire more staff, and build a city prevention infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: It is an investment worthy of our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The most diverse city in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That diversity is one of our greatest strengths, but it also requires intentional investments to keep those bonds strong.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Investments in relationship building, investments in education, investments in community trust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Today, I would like to highlight how OPHC, with the backing of the Mamdani administration, fulfills its mission to prevent hate, strengthen communities, and foster belonging across our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: No New Yorker should ever feel unsafe because of their identity, their beliefs,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: the appearances or background.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This administration had made that principle a foundation of its community safety agenda, and OPHC is proud to help carry it forward.

chapters.summary: Ramjatin describes the Partners Against the Hate Initiative (PATH), OPHC's backbone prevention infrastructure with six anchor organizations and 40+ sub-vendors providing culturally responsive programming in 20+ languages across all five boroughs. In FY2025, PATH conducted nearly 700 events engaging 62,000+ participants.
chapters.title: OPHC Director Ramjatin: PATH program overview and reach

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: One of the primary ways we accomplish this work is through Partners Against the Hate Initiative, or PATH.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: PATH is the backbone of New York City's community-based hate prevention infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Through six anchor organizations and more than 40 sub-vendors, PATH provides culturally responsive programming in more than 20 languages and reaches communities across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Because we believe hate prevention is not only about responding after harm occurs, PATH Health builds resilient communities by creating opportunities for

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: New Yorkers to connect, learn from one another, and recognize the common humanity that binds us all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: In fiscal year 2025 alone, through our PATH initiative and OPHC-led programming, we conducted nearly 700 events.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Engaged more than 62,000 participants across New York City, worked alongside over 240 partners, and in over 20 different languages.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: These efforts include anti-hate education, victim support services, conflict de-escalation training,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: youth programming, and outreach to communities that have historically faced barriers to reporting hate incidents.

chapters.summary: Ramjatin names PATH's six anchor partners including the 67th Precinct Clergy Council, JCRC, NY Anti-Violence Project, Arab American Association, Asian American Federation, and Hispanic Federation. He explains how trusted community organizations help prevent conflict escalation and build resilience against hate.
chapters.title: OPHC Director Ramjatin: PATH anchor partners and community impact

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: What makes PATH successful is that it is led by trusted organizations, deeply rooted in the communities they serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: These organizations are often the first places

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: residents turn to for support, information, and healing after experiencing bias or hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Co-part path partners, including the 67th Prison Clergy Council, the Jewish Community Relations Council, JCRC, the New York Anti-Violence Project, the Arab American Association of New York,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: the Asian American Federation, and the Hispanic Federation work together to combat anti-Semitism, anti-LGBTQ plus hate, Islamophobia, anti-Asian hate, anti-Arab hate,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: anti-Black racism, and other forms of bias while fostering inclusion, solidarity, and safety for all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The impact extends beyond any single event.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: When communities have trusted relationships, know where to seek help, and feel connected to one another,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: They are better equipped to prevent conflicts from escalating, support victims when incidents occur, and build resilience against hate and bias.

chapters.summary: Ramjatin describes the 7 on 7 initiative along Roosevelt Avenue in Queens, created during heightened community tension over street vending and quality of life concerns. The program brought together East Asian, South Asian, Southeast Asian, Latino, and LGBTQIA+ communities connected by the 7 train corridor to build unity and belonging.
chapters.title: OPHC Director Ramjatin: Unity in the Community 7 on 7 initiative

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Another way we accomplish this work is by bringing communities together through initiatives such as Unity in the Community 7 on 7.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This initiative

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: emerged during a period of heightened concern along Roosevelt Avenue in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Public conversations about street vending, quality of life concerns, and changing conditions in the corridor were creating tension among residents and community groups, threatening to erode trust and deepen division.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Rather than allowing communities to retreat further into silos, OPHC chose to create spaces for dialogue and connection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: OPHC and its partners brought together diverse communities connected by the seven train corridor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Seven on seven?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That's the whole thing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Including East Asia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: South Asia, Southeast Asian communities, Latino communities, LGBTQIA plus communities, and all diverse of other communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We partner with city agencies to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: to bring resources directly to neighborhoods while creating opportunities for residents to share experiences, learn about one another, our culture, and identify common concerns.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The goal was simple, to remind New Yorkers that what we have, that there is more in common that unites us

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: than dividers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: They initially demonstrated that power of bringing communities together by creating opportunities for residents to connect, share experiences, and build relationships.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: It helped strengthen trust, deepened understanding, and reinforced the sense of belonging that makes communities resilient.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: These connections are the foundation of community cohesion, helping neighbors see one another not as strangers, but as partners in building a strong and more connected community.

chapters.summary: Ramjatin presents YALP, launched October 2025 in partnership with NYC Public Schools, empowering youth ages 8-18 as ambassadors of inclusion. Participants receive training in leadership, civic engagement, public speaking, and restorative practices, then return to schools to lead projects and facilitate discussions.
chapters.title: OPHC Director Ramjatin: Youth Ambassador Leadership Program (YALP)

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Finally, as a critical part of our long-term prevention strategy is investing in young people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Through our Youth Ambassador Leadership Program, or YALP.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Launched in October 2025 and in partnership with the New York City Public Schools, the Youth Ambassador Leadership Program, or YALP, empowers young people between the ages of 8 and 18 to become ambassadors of inclusion, respect, and belonging.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The program brings together youths, parents, educators,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: mentors, and city leaders from across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Participants receive training in

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: leadership, development, civic engagement, public speaking, community organizing, and restorative practices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: What makes Yale particularly powerful is that young people are not simply learning about leadership, they are practicing it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Participants in the program return to their schools and communities to lead projects.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Facilitate discussions in their schools and communities, and serve as a positive role model for their peers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: At a time when many young people are exposed to misinformation, division, and online hate, YAL provides an opportunity to equip the next generation with the tools necessary to build stronger,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: more inclusive communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The young people we engage today will become the community leaders, educators, public servants, business owners, and parents of tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: By teaching empathy, leadership,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: civic engagement, and respect for differences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Now, we are investing in the long-term prevention of hate and bias before it takes root.

chapters.summary: Ramjatin summarizes OPHC's programming including interfaith engagement, youth art projects, community dialogues, and victim support. He outlines plans to deepen engagement in emerging communities, broaden culturally responsive programming, and expand PATH, YALP, and community project grants with the new funding.
chapters.title: OPHC Director Ramjatin: programming summary and future vision

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: All told, our programming at OPHC, which includes interfaith engagement, youth art projects, community dialogues, anti-hate education, and victim support,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Services help build trust, strengthen relationships across communities, support victims, and create opportunities for New Yorkers to engage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: cross differences before conflicts occurs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: As part of the Office of Community Safety and with the support of Mayor M.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Dani, Deputy Mayor Francois and the newly appointed OCS Commissioner, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Ayesha Delaney-Bramsey, OPHC is working closely with our partner offices to strengthen coordination, share data,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: and best practices and identify new opportunities to advance community safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Together, we are helping shape a coordinated approach to prevention that recognizes the important role community engagement, education, and relationship building play in keeping New Yorkers safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Mayim M.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Dani's historic investment in OPHC will allow us to expand and enhance our work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We are exploring ways to deepen engagement within emerging communities, strengthen community-based hate prevention efforts,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: broaden access to culturally responsive programming, and build upon successful initiatives such as the PATH program, the YALP program, and community project grants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Finally, I want to emphasize that preventing hate is not the responsibility of any one agency.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: It requires collaboration across government,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: schools, faith communities, nonprofit organizations, community leaders, and everyday New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We are fortunate to do this work under an administration that shares that belief.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: and has backed it with real attention and investment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: OPHC is proud to serve as a convener and connector in that work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Every day, we witness communities choosing understanding over division, dialogue over conflict, and unity over hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Those moments may not always make headlines, but they are the foundation of a safer and stronger New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Under Mayor Mamdani's leadership, we have a true opportunity to build a city where belonging is strengthened, communities are connected,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: and neighbors see one another as partners in a shared future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: By investing in prevention, we can address the conditions that allow hate to take root and create communities that are more resilient, inclusive, and safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Thank you for your continued partnership and support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I look forward to answering any questions you may have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'd like to recognize that we've been joined by Councilmember Darlene Mealy.

chapters.summary: Phylisa Wisdom, Executive Director of the Mayor's Office to Combat Antisemitism (MOCA), describes a five-borough listening tour finding that nearly every visibly Jewish person has personally experienced an antisemitic incident. She notes Jewish hate crimes make up over 50% of total hate crimes despite overall declines.
chapters.title: MOCA Director Wisdom: listening tour and rising antisemitic trends

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Salam and all esteemed members of the Committee to Combat Hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: My name is Felisa Wisdom and I am the Executive Director of the Mayor's Office to Combat Antisemitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: The Mamdani administration is deeply grateful to all of you for the work that you do, both in this committee and outside of it, to ensure that our city remains safe and welcoming for every single New Yorker, regardless of their background.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Our city is in a delicate moment, even amidst the joy that so many of us have felt this summer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: While hate crimes as a whole are on the decline, we see a distressing trend of rising hate crimes targeting the Jewish community, making up over 50% of the sum total.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: The Mayor's Office to Combat Antisemitism is in the midst of a listening tour with members of the Jewish community across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: While we've heard universal appreciation for the diversity of this city and its rich Jewish culture, we've also heard some distressing anecdotes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Nearly every single visibly Jewish person we've spoken to, for example, someone who wears a kippah or other religious garment, has said that they've personally been the victim of an anti-Semitic incident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: These incidents have left entire communities shaken, afraid to proudly do what they've done for years, to simply be who they are in the greatest city in the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Other New Yorkers who wear religious garments understand this fear.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Whether or not these rise to the level of crimes, they represent a tear in the fabric of our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: It's wrong, it's unacceptable, and it's something that my office...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: in collaboration with the NYPD, OPHC, City Commission on Human Rights, and the Office of Mass Engagement, and many other offices, up to and including the mayor himself, is working day in and day out to chip away at.

chapters.summary: Wisdom explains that MOCA was a six-month-old office with a single employee when the Mamdani administration entered. She has revitalized the interagency task force on antisemitism, meeting individually with nearly every member agency, and maintains strong communication with NYPD, OPHC, CCHR, and other partners.
chapters.title: MOCA Director Wisdom: office growth and interagency task force

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: When this administration entered City Hall, the Mayor's Office to Combat Antisemitism was a six-month-old office consisting of a single employee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: The work was not yet designed to stand the test of time beyond any one administration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: The mayor and our team have set out to remedy that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: I'm joined in this work by Deputy Director Josh Benjamin, and we are in the midst of growing our staff to at least one or two others.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: This office will be equipped to advance meaningful and proactive policy, programming, and messaging.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: With a goal to stand the test of time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: We've revitalized this office's interagency work, meeting individually with nearly every single member of the interagency task force to combat anti-Semitism, while also convening an initial group meeting to discuss how anti-Semitism arises in the contexts of various agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Previously, this task force had a single meeting,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: and members are enthusiastic about working together in pursuit of our shared goals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: We maintain strong, consistent, open lines of communication with NYPD, OPHC, CCHR, and Office of Mass Engagement and other relevant agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: We've also served as a facilitator for conversations on these issues with a variety of agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Our office has developed and retained close relationships with clergy, organizations, and other community leaders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: to understand how anti-Semitism impacts their day-to-day work and the New Yorkers they serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Moreover, our office, along with the Office of Mass Engagement, plays a critical role in constituent services, ensuring that Jewish communities around New York City are able to access functions of government, while also ensuring cultural competency across agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Our office seeks to combat anti-Semitism with a robust, whole-of-government approach, with education on the front end and enforcement on the back end.

chapters.summary: Wisdom describes developing what may be the first municipal strategy to combat antisemitism in the US, tracking for release by end of summer. She also launched a new website for public contact, noting the previous office had no website or way to be reached without existing connections.
chapters.title: MOCA Director Wisdom: municipal strategy and new website

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: As I mentioned in the beginning of my testimony, our office is deep in the throes of our listening tour, having met with scores of Jewish New Yorkers from across the religious and political spectrum.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: We are using these sessions in part to develop what may be the first municipal strategy to combat anti-Semitism in the United States.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: We're tracking to have this released by the end of the summer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Any New Yorker who's interested in joining a listening session or otherwise connecting with our office can reach out via the contact form on our website, which is at newyorkcity.gov, combat anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: On this note, the administration strongly believes that government should be accessible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Under the previous iteration of this office, there was not a website or way to contact the office unless you had an existing connection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Therefore, it was a priority for us to develop a website where New Yorkers could get in touch with us while also demonstrating details of some of the day-to-day work of our office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: The website will continue to be updated over the coming months and years, and we look forward to further developing and formalizing all of our communications practices.

chapters.summary: Wisdom highlights the massive influx of OPHC funding from $3M to $29M annually (886% increase) and emphasizes close communication with OPHC on how to best use the increased funding to combat antisemitism alongside all other forms of hate.
chapters.title: MOCA Director Wisdom: OPHC funding increase and closing remarks

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Finally, I'd like to thank the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: I'd be remiss if I did not mention what my colleague just mentioned, which is one of the most exciting priorities and accomplishments of the Mamdani administration thus far, the massive influx of funding into OPHC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: OPHC's funding has dramatically increased under this administration from $3 million annually to $29 million annually, an increase of 886%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: We are in close communication with OPHC and the Office of Community Safety broadly regarding how to best use this increased funding to meaningfully combat anti-Semitism alongside our administration's work to combat all other forms of hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Thank you again for inviting my colleagues and I to testify before this critical committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: I look forward to answering your questions and continuing our shared work over the coming years.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about OPHC's long-standing partnerships and selection process. Ramjatin explains the six anchor organizations identified since the office's 2019 founding, chosen for their bandwidth to deliver grassroots programming citywide across all five boroughs.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on community partnerships and selection process

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: All right, I'm going to first,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'd like to acknowledge as well that we have been joined by Councilmember Carl Wilson.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And I'm going to start with some questions, starting with...

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: The Office of the Prevention of Hate Crimes.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: OPHC prides itself on its work with community partners.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Could you speak to some of the long-term, long-standing partnerships that you have in the selection process for these partnerships?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: OPHC's PATH program is the vehicle where we work with over 60-plus organizations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But we have six major anchor organizations who we work with directly, and then they have sub-vendors under each of their portfolios.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Namely, we have the God Squad, 67% Christian Credit Council, JCRC, Jewish Council Relations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have the Arab American Association of New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have the Hispanic Federation, the New York City Anti-Violence Project, and we have the Hispanic Federation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And Asian American Federation, if I missed that one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Those are the six anchor organizations that we work with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Since the inception of this office in 2019, those groups were identified as the broader groups who can take the load of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: amplifying this work, but also to bring this work on a grassroots level across New York City diverse communities, and they have the bandwidth to do so citywide across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: You're welcome.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks how OPHC allocates funds and whether certain neighborhoods are prioritized. Ramjatin explains each anchor organization receives equal funding from the $3M base and sub-grants to vendors citywide, noting prior funding could not reach every community but the 800% increase will expand reach.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on funding allocation and neighborhood prioritization

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: How does OPHC allocate funds to organizations that it works with?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And are there some neighborhoods or community prioritized when it comes to funding?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And if so, what is that prioritization based on?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yes, so before my time in the office in 2019 when this office was founded, those were the organizations that were selected to be part of this programming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: After coming into this role, I realized that I noticed the great work they were doing and also to the diversity of portfolios that they have under each of the organizations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The way we allocate the funding is historically each organization receives the same amount of funding across the $3 million, and then they subvent to vendors across New York City to reach most neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Of course, with the prior funding, we could not reach every single community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That's why we welcome this administration's expanded funding by 800% to make sure that we are going to meet most of the communities in New York City going forward.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks which Queens, Brooklyn, and Manhattan areas were targeted and whether Staten Island and the Bronx are covered. Ramjatin reports programming in all five boroughs, including Community Project Grants of $10,000 each and seed grants for rapid response, such as Central Queens Against Hate for Forest Hills and Rego Park.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on underserved areas and five-borough coverage

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: In fiscal year 2025, OPHC worked towards reaching communities that are typically underserved by prevention services.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: For example, through the PATH program in 2025, OPHC reported engaging with five grassroots organizations in underserved areas of Queens, Brooklyn, and Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Which areas in Queens, Brooklyn, and Manhattan were targeted?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: How does OPHC determine whether an area is underserved?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And are there plans to target areas in Staten Island and the Bronx?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So I'm happy to report that we do have programming in all five boroughs across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So aside from the PATH program, OPSC has what's called, in partnership with the City Commission on Human Rights, we have a program called the Community Project Grants that we sought to fund

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: 10 organizations across New York City or individuals, anyone 14 years or older, with $10,000 each year to meet those needs that you mentioned before, the pockets that we feel like is missing out in the community grassroots work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But also, too, if we see an escalation, just this year, this past two months, we work with a group called Central Queens Against Hate because of the rise in hate crimes in Queens, specifically in the Forest Hills area, Regal Park

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: area, Kew Gardens area, and we pivot to make sure we give out seed grants as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So this past year and in 2025, we funded approximately...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: 12 different seed grant opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And this year, we gave out 14 seed grants to different community members across the city to meet their needs as hate crimes happen.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about the 7 on 7 series feedback and replication plans. Ramjatin reports positive community feedback, with six of seven events completed and a culminating event planned. He envisions expanding to other transit lines (5 on the 1s, 10 on the A's) and bus routes across all boroughs.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on 7 on 7 series feedback and expansion plans

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Can you tell us more about the Roosevelt Island 7 on 7 series and any initial feedback that you've received from the communities that are being served?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And has this project concluded or is engagement with these communities ongoing?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And are there plans to replicate the 7 on 7 model?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: By working with any other transit corridors, or by replicating any other successful aspects of this particular series.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I'm very proud of the 707 programming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The name is catchy, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we want to do five on the ones and 10 on the A's.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And, you know, one of the strengths of New York City is its diversity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I think if you go into any train cart in New York City, you will find the wealth of New York City's diversity on any cart.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Sadly, no one speaks to each other.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we have used the veins of the natural existing subway lines and bus routes to use those as connectors of communities, but more so to highlight.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That diversity, but also to bring those teams together.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The 707 project came about in a time when we saw heightened community disharmony along Roosevelt Avenue and different communities, you know, blaming each other for the street vending or whatever activities were happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we saw

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This is an opportunity to bring these communities together.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But more importantly, we wanted to bring communities who belong to one part of the world in one same room to build unity within that community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: For example, we brought together 12,000 people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Southeast Asian communities in one room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We brought together six different nations in one room for the East Asia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We brought together almost like 10 different countries, people for the Southeast, for the South Asia community, Breaking Bread, Building Bond initiative.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: These programs bring these communities together, first and foremost, to build belonging.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: To say, here, we see you, we appreciate you, and we value you and all you bring to New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This program is ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have completed six of the seven, seven, not seven, six of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And the final one is going to be where we bring all the communities together in one culminating one and say, we are one Roosevelt Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have heard positive things about this program in the sense where communities felt seen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: They felt respected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: They felt heard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And more importantly, city government as a whole is coming to their doors where we are bringing resources and saying, hey, how can we serve you?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: How can we make sure you feel seen, heard, and respected exactly as you are?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This is one of the priorities of this administration to make sure every New York City, and again, I'm thankful and I'm grateful for the Mayamam Dani's expansion by 800% in our office to make sure we can expand this program, of course, with staff as well, to every borough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we have five of these sessions happening all the time across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Staten Island only has one train, but we'll find other routes as well.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Gotcha.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about successful youth programs and feedback. Ramjatin describes NYC Youth Moving Forward Against Hate, launched January 2025, working with 4th-5th graders through high school and colleges. Post-surveys show 35%+ of students now know the difference between hate, bias, and discrimination.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on youth initiatives and school feedback

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: OPHC emphasizes the importance of working with our city's youth as key components in

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Hate prevention.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Which of your youth-based initiatives have seen particular success, and what do you attribute that to?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: In addition to that, what feedback have you received from the young people that participate in your programs and at the events?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And how is this feedback incorporated into future planning?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we have a young lady, her name is Zoe, and we had gone, so we started a program called NYC Youth Moving Forward Against Hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This initiative started in 2025, in January of 2025.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: When I came into this role, at that time, we saw a lot of college campuses experiencing heightened hate and bias and discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And my thought was that at some point, these college campus students were youths.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And I truly believe that the first time a child or a person knows what a hate crime is, is not when they commit one or when one is committed towards them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: They should know what the difference between bias, discrimination, and hate is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And more importantly, they should know that there are consequences for these actions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But also to build unity and alliance among these students at a very tender age.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So the NYC Youth Moving Forward Against Hate program, we are in schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And it was groundbreaking because before people said it couldn't be done because parents will rally against it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And we found a way to work with principals and school districts to say, how can we come to your schools and speak to the fourth and fifth graders so they leave with knowledge going to middle school about hate, bias, and discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: How can we speak to elementary students in the 7th and 8th grade and high school students in the 11th and 12th grade and expand this programming also to colleges?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And I'm proud to say we have worked with City College, City Tech in Brooklyn, to also bring this knowledge there too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And what we're doing, we're talking about hate, bias, discrimination, the difference between these things, how to recognize it, but more importantly, how we can build allyship.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: How can we appreciate the differences in our classrooms?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And what we have seen at this point is more than 35% of our students, and we do a pre- and a post-survey.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: To this programming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And we leave each student with one of these brochures that we have printed out that we've made.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have an elementary school brochure, we have a high school brochure, we have a middle school brochure, and an adult version.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And at the back of these brochures, there's a part to sign that says,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I pledge to help create a community where everyone is safe and accepted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And I pledge to defend my peers if they are being treated unfairly based on who they are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And I pledge to help New York City move forward against hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And each student gets one of these to sign, and they make an informal pledge like we pledge to you to tell the truth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And they say they're going to be a part of me to solve hate crimes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The point is this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This work cannot be done from my office sitting where I am.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: It has to be done on every single level, on the grassroots level, at the schools, at the mosques, at the synagogues, on the streets, at the bars, wherever we can go, we go.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And we ask each student to sign this portfolio, this flyer, and keep it with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Lastly, I'll see you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: that we've seen over 35% of students report post-survey that they now know the difference between hate, bias, and discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: More than 18% of them now know the difference between those three, what resources their school have, and more than 20% of them now know they could get in trouble if they continue to do hate, bias, and discrimination.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks how OPHC tracks school impacts like bullying and hateful graffiti. Ramjatin explains OPHC receives monthly reports from NYPD Hate Crimes Task Force on schools with increased bias incidents, then proactively reaches out. He notes they also work beyond NYC public schools, including an imam training school.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on tracking school impact and incident response

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: That's really, really excellent.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Young people are often, unfortunately, exposed to hate right in their very own schools, one of the places that they should feel the safest.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: So my question is, how does OPHC track the impacts of its work in schools, whether it be bullying, hateful graffiti, property damage?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Or hate-motivated violence, has any schools reported improvements as a result of the OPHC programming that has been conducted?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And also, has OPHC ever been contacted for guidance or education specifically in response to an incident at a school?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So again, OPSC's work is not enforcement in nature.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We are preventative.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we don't want anyone calling NYPD Hate Crimes Task Force in the first place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But we do get our reports from the NYPD Hate Crimes Task Force, who gives us a report quarterly, monthly, as I asked them to let me know which schools you are seeing increased.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Reported bias incidents happening on their premises.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And then we reach out to those schools and we say, hey, here's what we do, here's what we can do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: and help you to do this programming, and we go to the schools and we meet them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This program has been successful as we have received a lot of incoming requests to go to the schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have also gone beyond the New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This past year, we went to the largest imam training school in Hillside Avenue, where we educated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: All the students who were part of that school over there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we go beyond the New York City public schools, anywhere students are learning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We want to be also teaching about hate, bias, and discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The good thing is that, listen, as I mentioned again, I am super excited about the administration's investment in our office because while these are all programs we tested out,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: to see if they're working and what the impact is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Now we have the real backing, we have the real funding and the commitment from this administration to make sure we expand this success across the city.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks what MOCA covers, then raises the scholarly point that Arabs and Muslims are also Semitic. He questions whether anti-Arab and anti-Muslim hate should be included under antisemitism, noting these groups are often miscategorized into broader hate-based incidents.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions MOCA on scope and raises broader definition of antisemitism

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I want to move towards the mayor's office to combating anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Could you provide what combating anti-Semitism covers?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Sure, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So like my colleague, my office is not an enforcement office, but rather focused by executive order on prevention, policy, and programming to prevent anti-Semitic incidents and also

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: increase social cohesion across groups.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: As a diverse city, I'm concerned about anti-Semitic people writ large.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And of course, Arabs are ethnically Semitic.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Arabs and even Aramaic are Semitics, and these are Semitic languages, of course.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And what we've seen is that Judaism, Christianity, and Islam are all born in the Semitic region.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Considering that scholarly studies have shown that hate against Muslims are often categorized under race-based bias,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: We do not know the true numbers of anti-Muslim hate.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: On top of that, anti-Muslim hate are often not reported due to the 9-11 related fear.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: In parallel, Arabs are often ethnolinguistically or an ethnolinguistic group.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What I find interesting is that considering

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: the rise in hatred against Arabs, Muslims, and Jews, despite underreporting of such incidents, specifically with regards to the Arabs and Muslims,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: they continue to be miscategorized and thrown into a bucket of broader-based hate-based incidents instead of being considered equally.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: As anti-Semitism, despite being a Semitic of origin.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Can we expect that Mocha will incorporate these groups under the umbrella of anti-Semitism and then subcategorize them based on what origin, such as anti-Semitism against Jews, anti-Semitism against Muslims,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: and anti-Semitism, a little bit of a tongue twister, against Arabs, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So I agree with you that there are lots of forms of hate that are scary and rising and deserve an enormous amount of attention from our city government.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Anti-Semitism is widely understood across countries and academic...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Contexts to be prejudice, hatred, and violence against

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Jews because they're Jewish.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: I understand what you're saying about Semitic and what that word means, but the term anti-Semitism is widely understood to be against Jewish people because they are Jewish.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So in terms of how this administration combats the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: serious crises of anti-Arab and Islamophobic hate and bias incidents that is across other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: This agency, this office is focused on anti-Semitism and the safety and thriving of the Jewish community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: And across all of our offices and across City Hall, there is certainly focus.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: On those other communities, but I would, I guess, refer those questions to my colleagues who work on a broader hate and bias portfolio.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam pushes for NYC to lead in expanding the definition of antisemitism to include all Semitic peoples. Wisdom responds that the term is widely understood as anti-Jewish hatred and that the administration combats all forms of hate across offices, with no form privileged above another. Salaam suggests more inclusion is needed.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam and MOCA debate broader definition of antisemitism

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: No, I definitely appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I think that especially in a place like New York City, the most diverse place in the world, there definitely is an opportunity for us to be able to pull

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: that reality into the broader meaning of anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Because of course those folks who are here are saying to themselves, hold on, I'm of Semitic origin as well.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I speak Semitic languages, how come I'm not included?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And I think that to exclude them causes greater division.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Especially when we want to be able to, you know, I'm looking at what's going on around the world and the fact that countries have been able to begin tolerance.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I don't want to say experiments, but they've been doing tolerance exercises.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: It's been really, really great.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And I think America, specifically New York City, has an opportunity to really be a leader in that particular space.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: And I have colleagues in the room who I'm sure will talk when it's time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: We're not colleagues, community organizations, so I'm sure we'll talk about the definitions of those terms when it comes time for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: The Mamdani administration does understand the term anti-Semitism to be related particularly to the Jewish community, but also sees a responsibility to combat all forms of hate in solidarity with each other,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: no form of hate privileged above another when it comes to the focus and investment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: As you heard, $26 million additional dollars to the Office for Prevention of Hate Crimes, which is charged with prevention and combating hate across all identities in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: And we're very excited to be partnering with them and with the Office of Faith Partnerships, NYPD, CCHR, anyone who has a very broad purview and portfolio to combat hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: And that work is ongoing and taken very seriously.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Well, I can say this.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: As the chair to the Committee to Combat Hate, I do know that initially when we first started our hearings,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: the overwhelming sentiment was this was specifically only going to be talking about

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: anti-Semitism as been understood by the broader definition of anti-Jewish hate.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And of course, you know, as the chair, I'm supremely aware of my responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And also being able to have the melting pot, that kaleidoscope of the human family really being represented here, especially in city council.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: So maybe there's more to do and more to include as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about MOCA staffing and expansion plans. Wisdom reports two current staff growing to three or four, a 300-400% expansion. On outreach, she describes the ongoing listening tour across five boroughs and interfaith cooperation as central to the Mamdani administration's approach.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions MOCA on staffing and community outreach efforts

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: How many people are currently on staff at the mayor's office to combat anti-Semitism?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Currently, there are two staff members, and we, depending on the outcomes of the executive budget, will grow to three or four.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Given that religion-based hate crimes generally and anti-Semitic hate crimes specifically are consistently among the most reported hate crimes, are there currently any plans to expand MOCA?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: MOCA is experiencing expansion of 300% to 400% from the previous administration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So, yes, we are expanding.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And we've heard from some of our constituents.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Okay, gotcha.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Could you tell us about MOCA's outreach and community engagement efforts?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What does MOCA on the ground work look like?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And do you partner with any community-based organizations, and are there any efforts being made towards interfaith cooperation and engagement?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So to the last part, yes, there are interfaith.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: We believe strongly in the Mumdani administration that combating hate is a group project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: And while each form of hate, Islamophobia, homophobia, anti-Semitism is unique, that we are all impacted by hate in our city and need to be thinking about combating hate across all identities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: In terms of community organizations, as you heard in my testimony, this office is really very new.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So in terms of the norms of our day-to-day work, we're building those right now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: And the first...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Order of Business is a listening tour where we're talking with Jewish New Yorkers and people who work in the space to combat anti-Semitism across

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: the religious and political spectrum, across all five boroughs, to understand the work that they do, what the people that they serve are experiencing, and then we'll make a first round of recommendations in our report coming out at the end of the summer, and we'll continue to iterate on that report year upon year.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about MOCA's work increasing dialogue among different Jewish communities. Wisdom acknowledges the Jewish community is divided and explains MOCA's role is ensuring every group has a seat at the table with the administration and their experiences as Jews are seen as legitimate.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions MOCA on intra-Jewish community dialogue

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: New York City is home to the largest Jewish population outside of Israel.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And Jewish New Yorkers are not just one community, but many.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What kind of work is MOCA doing in relation to increasing dialogue and understanding amongst the different Jewish communities inside of New York City itself?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: It's true that there is both the work to increase intergroup understanding between groups, as well as in the moment we find ourselves, the Jewish community is quite divided, and there is work to be done to increase cohesion

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: among the Jewish community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: The role of the mayor's office to combat anti-Semitism in that work, as opposed to communal organizations who do communal work, is to ensure that every single group has a seat at the table

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: and has a relationship with the administration and their voices heard and their experiences as Jews are seen as legitimate and taken seriously.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about MOCA's restorative justice work, interagency collaboration, and startup challenges. Wisdom reports MOCA has not yet done restorative justice work but supports partner agencies doing so. She describes convening the interagency task force and sees building the new office as an opportunity rather than a challenge.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions MOCA on restorative justice, interagency work, and startup challenges

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: This is a follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Has MOCA done any work in the restorative justice space, and how much does MOCA focus on?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
