utterances.text: on education as a tool for rehabilitation?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: MOCA thus far hasn't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Again, previously, you know, we were sort of picking up a very new office and looking at what that work will look like throughout the administration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: I know that our colleagues are, you know, PHC in particular, but also the entire administration, New York City Public Schools, other agencies are thinking very seriously about how to infuse restorative justice practices in their work, and we will support them in that.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Can you describe for us in more detail about how MOCA collaborates with other offices or agencies?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What portion of MOCA's work focuses on interagency collaboration and coordination versus direct engagement with the communities?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So one of the things that we are tasked with doing by executive order is to convene an interagency task force on anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So that task force, which we have picked up, again, I had previously met once, so there was an enormous set of norms to follow through with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: But we are meeting with representatives from each agency and ensuring that there is a feeling of a cohesive working group on this topic that is regularly meeting, sharing best practices with each other, and infusing this work into the rest of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: the work the agency is doing to combat hate of all forms.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And MOCA is a relatively new office.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Are there any startup challenges or questions you are still facing?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: I think in a new administration in general, we're all figuring out what our offices look like in this admin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: This office in particular, as you noted, is just over a year old.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: And so we're really building its work in some ways from scratch.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: Recommend taking the report that was left for us before, but really building up policies and practices that make sense within the Mamdani administration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: So I don't think we're seeing it as a challenge.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: I think we're seeing it as an opportunity to be really thoughtful and deliberate about what kinds of policies and programs and budget allocations would best serve our diverse communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: And I think

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Phylisa Wisdom
utterances.text: After year one, when we've been through a budget and we are fully staffed, we'll just continue to see this work grow and iterate.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Absolutely.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam notes OFCP's website was minimal and recently taken down, asking how the office engages without a public website. Executive Director Ali Atif explains OFCP operates under the Office of Mass Engagement and the public can contact them through OME's website.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OFCP on public presence and website

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I want to move towards the Mayor's Office of Faith-Based and Community Partnerships.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: The Mayor's Office of Faith-Based and Community Partnerships was established in 2022 by the previous administration with the purpose of connecting city agencies with faith communities throughout New York City.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: However, there is little public information about your work.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: The office's previous website

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: was very minimal to begin with, and it was recently taken down.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: My questions are, does the OFCP plan to establish a more public presence, and how does the OFCP currently engage with communities with

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: without having a public website.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Salam, and thank you for the entire committee for allowing me to participate on this panel with my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: My name is Ali Atif, and I'm the Executive Director at the Office of Faith-Based Partnerships, and thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: The Office of Faith-Based Partnerships is under the auspice of the Office of Mass Engagement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: And so every New Yorker can get in contact with our office through the Office of Mass Engagement's website.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks for examples of OFCP's policies and programs. Atif describes working with congregations and nonprofits across all five boroughs, including distributing 30,000 Know Your Rights flyers with MOIA, hosting an interfaith breakfast for 400 faith leaders, and coordinating cooling center partnerships with NYCEM.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OFCP on programs and partnerships

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: OFCP generally works to support policies and programs related to social welfare, public safety, and community engagement.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Could you give us some examples of such policies and programs and the groups OFCP has worked to support?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We work very closely with local congregations, nonprofits across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We have the capacity to liaise with all communities across ideological spectrums.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: It's really important to us that everyone has access to government and that there's proper follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: So we are big tent and welcome anyone who might want to work with us or who needs services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: So we have a policy of come as you are and we will meet our communities where they're at.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: Specifically, in terms of programs and partnerships, we want to integrate faith partnerships in every aspect of OMI, as well as this administration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: So to that end, we've worked closely with the Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs to give out more than 30,000 Know Your Rights flyers on how to protect yourself during interactions with federal agents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: At our first event, which was our interfaith breakfast attended by more than 400 faith leaders, we hosted an information session with faith leaders from 2K child care districts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: the Office of Child Care and Early Childhood Education, to seek their partnership in helping connect families to free, highly

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: quality early childhood education opportunities in their communities, in collaboration with the Interfaith Center of New York and Union Theological Seminary.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We coordinated a preliminary briefing on the city's racial equity plan with the Mayor's Office of Equity and Racial Justice for faith leaders to give their input on that plan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We shared New York City Emergency Management's request.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: To hundreds of houses of worship to consider serving as cooling centers in moments of heating emergencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We have hosted roundtables and meetings with faith partners as part of a listening tour to better understand their issues as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks whether OFCP engages with the nearly one-third of New Yorkers who are religiously unaffiliated. Atif says faith leaders are defined broadly and they welcome anyone leveraging faith-based values for social good. He also describes an ongoing listening tour across houses of worship citywide.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OFCP on engaging unaffiliated New Yorkers and future projects

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Some anti-religious biases are not from

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: other religious groups, but rather from non-religious communities that have a negative view on one religion or

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: just religion broadly.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Nearly a third of New Yorkers do not affiliate with any religion.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Does OFCP engage with or have any plans to engage with unaffiliated New Yorkers to promote interfaith understanding?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: Thank you so much for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We define faith leaders broadly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: There are individuals who we are engaging with across ideological spectrums who may be unaffiliated with the house of worship, but still identify and leverage their faith-based values for social good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We welcome all and everyone who wants to partner with us.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And lastly, are there any special projects on the horizon for OFCP?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you so much for that question as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: As I mentioned, we're currently on a listening tour.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We want to make sure that decisions about community include community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We have gone across the city visiting houses of worship and faith leaders, and we look forward to those partnerships.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about OFCP's structure, staff size, and hiring. Atif reports four staff including himself, planning a fifth hire, and expresses excitement about being part of the Office of Mass Engagement with the mayor's prioritization of faith partnerships.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OFCP on staffing and resources

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Actually, that was second to last.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: This is the last part.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Have there been any changes to your office's structure since the start of the current administration?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And how many dedicated staff does OFCP have?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Are you currently hiring?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And do you feel your resources are adequate in order to successfully complete your goals?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: Thank you so much for those questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: I hope I asked through all of them, and if I don't, please let me know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We currently have three staff members, four including myself.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: We are looking to have a fourth in the summer, and we are actively hiring for a fifth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: Could you ask the follow-up question?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Well, you did say you were currently hiring, so that's probably a yes.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Do you feel your resources are adequate in order to successfully complete your goals?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: Yes, we are really excited to be part of the Office of Mass Engagement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: The office is incredibly committed to faith partnerships.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: This office is something that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ali Atif
utterances.text: The mayor has prioritized, and as the executive director of this office, I'm dedicated to make sure that faith partnerships is part of every aspect of this mayoralty.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it to my colleagues for their questions.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: If you have any.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'm going to start with Darlene.

chapters.summary: CM Darlene Mealy asks whether Christians and African American clergy experience hate crimes, noting bishops face harassment on social media. Ramjatin responds about the 67th Precinct Clergy Council (God Squad) as a PATH partner and describes the Interfaith Council on Words and Symbols of Peace and Hate.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OPHC on Christian and African American clergy hate crimes

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I'm glad that we have the...

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: this committee because so many things are going on right now.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: But what is very alarming to me, could you explain, do you think Christians do not get harassed?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: faith-based Christians.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Because we have bishops that say they anti this, anti that, and on Facebook they get harassed.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And I haven't heard anything about African American Christians dealing with hate crime also.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Could anyone explain that to me?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Where do the African American clergy fit into this hate crime?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Because Catholics get

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So I hear everything else.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I just want to make sure that we're putting that faith base in this scenario.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And strong.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Don't everybody answer at one time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Sure, I can.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And I would like to, if you could please give to the council members one of these books, I'd appreciate this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This has a report that has a very deep description of all our programmings and our partners that we work with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: One of the partners that we work with is specifically the 67th Presbyterian Theology Council that is based in Brooklyn, New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And they work with the clergy specifically and a lot of programs in Brooklyn where they amplify programmings in the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: communities and with faith-based partnerships as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Our office also has what's called the Interfaith Council on Words and Symbols of Peace and Hate, where we have diverse faith leaders in that space where we work with them specifically to provide programming and we give seed grants to these.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Anyone who comes to us and say, hey, we're experiencing bias, discrimination in our church, in our faith, and we give them programming funding to do that, those in the communities.

chapters.summary: CM Mealy challenges OPHC's claim of coverage in her district covering Bed-Stuy, Brownsville, East New York, and Crown Heights, saying the God Squad only holds one or two meetings there. Ramjatin clarifies the six anchor organizations and their sub-vendors operate citywide, with significant Brooklyn programming.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OPHC on Brooklyn programming coverage gaps

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Do you know I cover 67%?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Pardon me?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I cover 67% of God's squad.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: But that's in Crown Heights, East Flatbush.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I have a big part of Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brownsville, East New York, Ocean Hill.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Half of those programs do not come into that area.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: God Squad don't really talk to the pastors there.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: They have one or two meetings and that's it.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So I hope going forward, I definitely want to know where these six programs come from.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: You said 67th.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And where is the one in the middle?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: East Flatbush?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: No, ABC?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: What is your six anchor organizations?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we have the COD squad, the 67th Patient Culture Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have Asian American Federation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have Arab American Association of New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have the Hispanic Federation, the Anti-Violence Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And JCRC.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Which one of them are in?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Like Bedford-Stuyvesant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So of the 51...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Council districts in the city, we are in half of them across the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We are in half of them across the city, and we have a lot of our programming in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So 67 precincts for practically all Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brownsville, East New York, Crown Heights, Canarsie.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Yeah, they really do Canarsie.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Bushwick.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: That's what you're saying?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yeah, so they're the anchor partner.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And I have one more question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And then they have sub-vendors, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So they have different, like six sub-vendors that are citywide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So while they may be based in Brooklyn, the programming takes place across the city, and the same is true for all the other organizations as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The programming takes place across the city on all five boroughs, regardless of where they're based in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Our request for each of these organizations is to make sure the programming is not only centered in the community, but also make sure that diversity is within the portfolio as well.

chapters.summary: CM Mealy asks about the hate crime notification system required under Local Law 49 of 2020, noting she only learns of hate crimes from precinct texts. Ramjatin says notifications go to community boards, CBOs, and elected officials within 24 hours of NYPD notification and promises to verify Mealy is on the list.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OPHC on hate crime notification system under Local Law 49

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So I would love to really get into this because somehow or another it's not being crossed the board.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And we cannot leave anyone out.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And one thing I wanted to ask you about was your program with under local law 49 of 2020.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: OPHC is also required to create a hate crime notification system, which notifies the community boards,

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: community-based organization, and elected officials of violent hate crimes in their area.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: How often do you use that?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Because I know only time I found out about a hate crime in my community, that's if the precinct texts me.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So what outlet do y'all have that y'all give to the community board?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And it trickled down to the elected officials and community-based organizations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yes, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Instead of the webpage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: For sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So whenever we receive notifications from NYPD that a hate crime has happened, we then go ahead and within 24 hours of receiving that notification, we send out a notification to all the groups you just mentioned there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We will triple check and double check to make sure that you're on that list to make sure that you get those notifications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And also, too, if there's anyone else you want to let us know to include, we will be happy to include them in that list as well.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: But I'm just saying, if you say y'all do this, it should automatically come to our city council emails.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: True or not?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: It's supposed to be coming there, and it should be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So I will triple check to make sure that it is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Please do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And ensure that you're added.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Can I just ask one more question?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: This, please, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I got to ask.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: CM Mealy questions why only Roosevelt Avenue was chosen for the 7 on 7 series, asking about the 2, 3, 4, 5, A, and C train lines with similar issues. Ramjatin explains the program was piloted due to heightened community division on Roosevelt Avenue and plans to expand to all train lines and bus routes with new funding.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OPHC on 7 on 7 series location selection

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Can you explain to me the 7 on 7 series?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Initial feedback, you say it's seven on seven, it's closest to the train stations.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: How did you pick these seven on seven?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Just Roosevelt Island, different, why not other locations?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Do you think the two or the three train does not?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Do you think the A or the C train does not have hate crimes?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Or you just feel it was more in Roosevelt Island?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Because I had on Easton Parkway, four to five.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Vendors was...

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Everywhere.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: The community was mad, the vendors was mad, everyone trying to make money.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So why wasn't considered the four to five line?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: You know, I want to just highlight two things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Within capacity, we can only do what we can.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Now that we've increased funding coming to the office, our goal is to, and this administration has made it very clear, that the investment that they're going to give to us is going to expand these programs that have been working across the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We piloted that program last year to get feedback, to learn what works, what does not work, and specifically Roosevelt Avenue, because we've seen a heightened sense of community division within that community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Our PATH program partners do work across New York City already.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So now that this program has been successful, we do have intentions to exactly follow the whole of the four train, the whole of the five train, and go through each of the lines.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: To the three train.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Two, three, five, A, C, one, C, E, all of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Z, we're going to be doing all of them over time.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And the J and the M.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: J and the M.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And the hate crimes we have over there on that line, that should have been the first one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And you'll be happy to know this, and bus lines too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Because we know Queens don't have many trains, so we're going to bus lines as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The point is this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We're going to use the natural lines that's already created where people connect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: To bring them even more close in the room, but also to bring that diversity in one room and say, hey, we are all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Please remember that.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So I would definitely be looking forward to it because

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So many different hate crimes that I don't know how I was just picked for Queens and Brooklyn is like the epic center.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So I look forward to making sure I get those notices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And I'll schedule a meeting with your office to make sure we can learn and hear more about you and tell you about the work that we're doing as well.

chapters.summary: CM Mealy asks how to find out who received seed grants and requests examples in Brooklyn. Ramjatin names grantees including Julia Keto, Red Lotus, and Christina Blackin, all African American individuals in Brooklyn. He notes applications increased 300% and offers info sessions to help organizations navigate the application process.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OPHC on seed grants in Brooklyn neighborhoods

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And how will we find out who got those seed grants?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So let me just discuss two things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The community project grants, their applications go out every year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we have everyone, this past year, we had applications increased by 300% from the previous year.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Could you name one, for example, in...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Bed-Stuy, Brownsville, anywhere in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Julia Keto, the new, the Red Lotus.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have Christina Blackin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: These are all African-American individuals who live in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: New organizations, too.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: New organizations, because the idea is innovative work, right?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Don't forget the old for the new, also.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Absolutely not.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So anyone applies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I mean, we'll meet, we'll talk more, and we want to expand these programs to make sure that we are giving every New Yorker who have an idea an opportunity to be part of the solution.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I will be part of that.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, for extending that time.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: My pleasure.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I think that this is important, too, because oftentimes what we've been finding, I think this is probably throughout all of our districts, is that there's been a little bit of a mystification of how to get through the process.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And so we want to make sure that those organizations that have been doing the work without being funded

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: at least get the education that allows them to be able to apply and participate with regards to city funding?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I mean, I'll add really quickly that just by the mere fact that after the first year we had a 600% increase in applications,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That means that we're making sure that every New Yorker we meet everywhere knows how to apply for these grants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And our office takes an extra role to make sure we educate even those who don't know how to apply.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have info sessions say, here are the how to break down the red tape and how you can apply for these seed grants and also the community project grants that's ongoing every year.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Councilman Bacar?

chapters.summary: CM Shekar Krishnan asks OPHC to elaborate on efforts combating hate in the LGBTQ community. Ramjatin describes working with the Anti-Violence Project, seed grants for LGBTQ organizations, and the 7 on 7 initiative highlighting the trans community on Roosevelt Avenue where profiling was occurring.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions OPHC on LGBTQ hate prevention efforts

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I have a couple of questions also for the Office of Prevention of Hate Crimes.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you for the report.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I look forward to digging into that a bit more.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: But if you wouldn't mind just elaborating, you spoke in your testimony about efforts in the LGBTQ community.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm wondering if you could elaborate a little bit more specifically about what efforts are being made to combat hate in that community specifically.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And the LGBTQIA plus community we know have historically been the group who has always faced hate crime.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: While we've seen hate crime for other groups fluctuate based on geopolitical activities, et cetera, one crime that we have seen consistently over time as the second most highest reported hate crimes, anti-male homosexual hate crimes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But we also know, Councilmember, that not all hate crimes are reported.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we have to go places where we think they're happening, but also, too, I always say my job, 99% of my job is to listen to the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And we work very closely with the Anti-Violence Project of New York City, they call it AVP, and they have been doing incredible work, working with partners across the New York City, like Cat and Lord, and

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: various other community groups to make sure that we are educating New Yorkers about the needs, the likes, dislikes of this community, but also to resources.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Hate prevention is not only standing up and saying, we stand with you, but also care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Being there to show care and to provide the resources that these New Yorkers feel seen, heard, and supported as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Additional to the PATH program that we have these different partners, we also work closely by giving out seed grants to a bunch of different community partners to make sure that we are making sure that the LGBT community is at the forefront.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Part of the 7 on 7 initiative, when we do the LGBTQIA plus community, we highlighted the trans community because we know erasure has been at the forefront recently.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So this program was centered in Roosevelt Avenue, exactly where a lot of profiling was happening towards the LGBTQIA plus community on Roosevelt Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have a lot of programming in Manhattan right now for the Pride Parade, Pride programs, and we are going to be amplifying that again with the new funding coming in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This mayor's commitment to prevention has been very clear, and he has made it very clear over and over that, not by words, but by action, saying we're going to invest in 800%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: increase in your office to ensure that we can serve many more partners across New York City.

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks how OPHC determines where hate is happening. Ramjatin explains they combine NYPD data with community listening, noting not all hate crimes are reported. He says LGBTQ hate crimes often occur on trains and in schools, and OPHC responds to both data and community requests.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions OPHC on data-driven approach to identifying hate

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: You said you have to go where you think they're happening, and you mentioned, I think, a Roosevelt Island example just there.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: How are you figuring out where they're happening?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We were listening.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Data-driven?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Are there– well, is it by data reporting or just anecdotally?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: you're hearing of it or how does that work?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So the fact that we know, let's underscore that question by saying not all hate crimes are reported.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So if we go by the data alone, we're missing half of the whole picture.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we go with the data of reporting and saying where the hate crimes happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Mostly the hate crimes happen for the LGBTQIA plus community is on the trains.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: A lot of times we see them in public transportation, and we have programs across the city that we are in the trains, we are in the streets, but we also see them in schools as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So part of the youth program that we have is working with the communities in the school to share about diversity, inclusion, equity, but also, too, we are also listening to the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we have programs in schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have programs in senior centers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Just this past week, actually, we have a member who called us and asked us, hey, I'm part of the LGBTQ community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: How can I support your office?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We are seeing hate crimes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And I'm proud to see my two interns who are here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: They are working with him directly to plan a Breaking Bread Building Bonds initiative in July.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So the point is that we listen to the community, to what they tell us, and we go to where they need us to be in the city.

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks how OPHC addresses homophobia and transphobia that manifests in people's homes among family members. Ramjatin acknowledges they cannot know about home incidents unless reported, then works with AVP and Voces Latina to provide culturally responsive outreach and support.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions OPHC on LGBTQ hate in private homes

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Oftentimes, homophobia, transphobia can manifest in people's homes, like not in public.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Places among family members.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So I'm wondering how you're thinking about combating LGBTQIA plus hate or violence or discrimination that's happening in folks'home and not in public places.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we would not know what happens in the homes unless it's reported.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And then once they are reported, we work with our partners, the Anti-Violence Project of New York, very closely to find out programming and the relevant partners within that community outreach to go in there and work with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have a group called Voces Latina, who is under Hispanic Federation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So even though we have the Anti-Violence Project of New York,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: This portfolio is not only within that one organization.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have many groups who does LGBTQIA plus work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And Voices Latina works specifically with LGBTQIA plus communities in Queens and across the city to ensure that their concerns, like you mentioned, are heard, but their services provided to support them as they need them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Next year, we have a new group where we have LGBTQIA plus youths.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That we're working with them who may be experiencing the fears, the distress at home, but a way to overcome that through education as well.

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks about collaboration with the Mayor's new Office of LGBTQIA+ Affairs. Ramjatin describes the Unity Grants program, where the LGBTQIA+ office identifies seven groups receiving $20,000 each through OPHC, a program ongoing for three to four years.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions OPHC on collaboration with Mayor's LGBTQIA+ office

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I just have one more quick question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Just curious if there's any collaboration, particularly with the LGBTQ hate prevention, with the mayor's new office of LGBTQIA plus affairs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have a project called Unity Grants that we work very closely with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So this office works through our...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: office to identify seven different LGBTQIA plus groups where we give $20,000 each.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And this program has been ongoing for the past three, four years now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And every year we have groups, many applications coming in to make sure to do this work in the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And again, I want to reiterate, with the expanded funding, we can do more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And we thank me and my family for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks which OPHC programs would be prioritized for scaling with the proposed FY2027 budget increase. Ramjatin identifies PATH as the top priority for expanding trusted partners beyond the current 60 organizations, plus youth initiatives and restorative justice programming for justice-involved individuals.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on budget priorities for scaling up

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I just have a few more questions.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Before we open it up to public testimony?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: So we know that the FY 2027 budget has not yet been finalized.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: The administration proposed that historic increase, as you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: My question is, what OPHC programming up until now has been the most successful that might be prioritized for scaling up if more resources, in fact, do become available?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So I would say all four programs have been successful because they all reach different New Yorkers at different points in their lives and across the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: However, I would say the PATH initiative, we have trusted partners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Again, these six anchor organizations and most of the sub-vendors who have been doing this work is definitely a place where we have to continue to support these trusted partners

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: on the ground to make sure we increase their funding and give them the resources to serve New Yorkers in the most appropriate way, but to expand this outreach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I don't think the PATH program should only have 60 organizations that serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: 8.5 million New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We need to have an expanded part where we reach Canarsie, we embed for Syverson and have more than one organization to meet the needs of New Yorkers across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So that's one program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We also want to expand our youth initiatives by making sure we have the appropriate staff to mobilize and to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: include more programming for the youths in schools, but also to include programming that has justice-involved individuals who have committed hate crimes to have a part where they can be rehabilitated and understand the hurt they have caused through restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: justices before they reenter the community.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks how OPHC responds to spikes in specific communities like anti-Buddhist and anti-female hate crimes. Ramjatin describes using seed grants for rapid response, including working with Bridging Cultures Group led by Dr. Debbie Almontaser to bring diverse women together after Islamophobic incidents against women.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on responding to spikes in anti-Buddhist and anti-female crimes

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Amidst an overall decrease in hate crimes across the city last year, we did see spikes in crimes against certain communities, including anti-Buddhist crimes and hate crimes against women.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: How does OPHC respond to this type of trend, and what does your work with community groups look like, and how does OPHC engage with other agencies?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Part of our initiative is exactly those seed grants that I mentioned to you before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Those seed grants are specifically geared to meet the moment when we do see spikes of hate crimes across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Just this past year, in the first quarter of this year, we saw an increase in Islamophobic hate crimes towards women, but interestingly committed by women, against women.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And immediately we sprung into action and we worked with a group called Bridging Cultures, Inc., Bridging Cultures Group, Inc., who is led by Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Debbie Almontesar.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And this program brings diverse females, any female-identifying person, into one room where we are building allyship, we're learning about leadership skills, and also inclusion and equity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: These programs, such as this one, is how we meet the moment when hate crimes do fluctuate across communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Additionally, as I mentioned before, we're working with a group called Central Queens Against Hate, where we are providing community access and resources to meet the moment of the rise in hate crimes in Queens as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about OPHC's recent relocation from the Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice to the Office of Community Safety. Ramjatin says there is no shift in approach, but the move enables a more holistic citywide approach with four additional partner agencies and better governance over resource allocation.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on relocation to Office of Community Safety

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Moving to interagency collaborations and coordination.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: OPHC was recently relocated from the Mayor's Office of Criminal Justice to the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Does OPHC foresee any corresponding shifts in its approach to hate prevention?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: For example,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Does that move reflect a shift in focus to engage with communities in a different way or strengthen your relationship with any particular city agencies?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The shift, there's no shift.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The shift has been, there's no shift.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The point is that this work has always been central to New York City Office of Prevention of Hate Crimes, where we are focusing on all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: OCS and moving to that office enables us to do more as a whole city approach, where now we are working with four more other agencies to bring a holistic approach to hate crime prevention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: And moving into this office now gives us much more lateral overview to make sure no communities are being missed, but also do we have governance to make sure that we are using resources widely to support New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Gotcha.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks how often the Interagency Committee meets and what topics are addressed. Ramjatin reports quarterly meetings with 20+ agencies discussing hate, bias, discrimination trends and coordinated city responses. He notes commissioners and mayoral staff attend meetings.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on Interagency Committee on Hate Crimes

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: OPHC oversees the Interagency Committee on Hate Crimes.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Part of this work is advising the mayor and implementing a unified citywide approach to hate crime prevention, awareness, investigations, prosecution, and community impact.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: How often does the committee meet?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And what are the primary topics addressed?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So the Interagency Committee on Hate Crimes, which I'm proud to say that our two partner agencies are part of, we meet on a quarterly basis, and we have over 20-plus agencies who are part of that committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: When we meet together, we talk about hate, bias, discrimination, and where it's happening, but also to collectively as a city, how can we respond to them?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And also, how often is the mayor directly involved in the interagency committee's work?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: The mayor and his staff, they often come to different staff members from the agencies, come to our meetings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Different commissioners come to the meeting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: They share what their offices are doing, but also listen to what work is being done by OPHC as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks about OPHC staff titles, expansion plans, and who sits on the Interagency Committee. Ramjatin reports three staff (Executive Director, Deputy Director, Senior Programs Manager), plans for borough-based liaisons, and that all OPHC staff participate in the Interagency Committee meetings.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions OPHC on staffing, civil service titles, and committee membership

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And moving to resources, we heard in the budget hearing that OPHC currently has three staffers.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Are there plans to increase that number of staff?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I know you probably answered that before, but like how much?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I want to answer that again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That's important.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Please.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We want to be in Carnassi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We want to be in Bedford-Stuyvesant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But, you know, we can only do so much with what we have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I, again, want to reiterate with the increased funding to our office and the commitment from this mayor and this administration to hate prevention, we definitely plan to increase our office's staff and to make sure we can do this work in the most coherent way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: What are the civil service titles of OPHC's current staff, and are there any you are looking to have added?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So at this point, you have my...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: who's the executive director.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: You have a deputy executive director and you have a senior programs manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We do plan to, you know, in collaboration and discussion with the new Office of Community Safety to have borough-based liaisons and to have, you know, because again, this work cannot be done from our office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: It has to be done on the ground in the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we feel having more eyes and ears on the ground in each borough, we can meet the needs of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Are staff dedicated to specific initiatives or do they work across multiple programs?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: For example, are there dedicated staffers for the path forward that are different than those for 7 on 7 or the Youth Leadership Ambassador Program?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Yes, so the way how we organize the work in the office is the staff members have a portfolio of different programs, but again, it's a holistic approach, so all of us know what's happening at all times across different programs.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And lastly, who from OPHC sits on the Interagency Committee on Hate Crimes, and what are their titles?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So interestingly, all my staff sits on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We all sit there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Of course, I lead the meetings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: My deputy director, Ms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Erica Ware, she also is the one organizing the meetings and having different speakers come to different meetings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But all our staff are part of those meetings.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'll pass it back to Councilmember Mealy.

chapters.summary: CM Mealy asks how OPHC works with schools and NYPD when bullying incidents occur, sharing a personal story of staff children being bullied. She questions whether principals should be mandated to report incidents and asks about coordination with the Department of Education to ensure reporting compliance.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OPHC on school incident reporting and DOE coordination

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Okay, I have just two questions.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: How has OPHC ever been contacted for guidance or education specifically in response to an incident at a school?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Could you give me an example?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Because I know one already.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I just had me stopping into my precinct, and I went in.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I saw some young kids sitting there, and I said, hi.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I didn't know them that well.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Find out it was my staff children, and they were there because they were bullied.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And the mother, she was on her way there to press charges.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So an incident like that.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: How would your program work with the police department to give that data that we desperately need?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: You say that y'all work with the police department, so could y'all give me how it trickles down?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Or do they call you and tell you?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I would love to know how this works.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So on our website, we have links that anyone who wants to request our office to come.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: That's the only way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: That's not the only way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So again, we are at the schools as well where we work with the New York City Public Schools to share out information across the whole city via the Respect for All Liaisons, through the guidance counselors, through the district.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: leaderships to all the schools that are in our offices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We also have the Heartwork Against Hate programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: So we try to make sure that a lot of schools and all schools know all the programs that our office offer and how they can get in touch with us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Again, we're doing this work based on capacity and we plan to increase and continue to do this across the city in a larger, more direct way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: But I want to answer this question to you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Any school can reach out to our office, and we can get involved with them to do this.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So it's up to the school.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So how are you working with the Board of Education to let them know that shouldn't the principals report it?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: To NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And then?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: But sometimes the principals do not.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: If my staff didn't follow through with it, it would never have been a case.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So it's up to the principals now to at least report it.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So are you working with Board of Education to make sure, or maybe we've got to put some legislation, that it's a mandate that they report it?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Because we need this information with bullying, any other thing.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: That is harmful regarding hate.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So that's something I got to think about.

chapters.summary: CM Mealy raises tensions between long-term residents and gentrifiers, asking if OPHC has considered hate or bias stemming from displacement fears. Ramjatin says OPHC listens to community concerns and will provide resources and support wherever called upon, and offers to meet with Mealy's office.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OPHC on gentrification-related hate and displacement tensions

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And do you know that...

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: It's kind of a tension between people who have been living here all this time with the gentifiers.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So do you think you could start doing anything in regard?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: To that because a lot of people feel they are being displaced.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: That's an issue.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And you never know how many people feel put aside, upset, and sometimes biased.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So therefore, have you ever thought about that hatred or putting something in place for that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: You know, we're always...

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Did you really know of it first?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Not specifically for justification, but again, we are in partnership with over 20 plus agencies across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have the hate crimes notification system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have our partners against the hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We have many programs across different levels.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Again, anyone, 90% of my job is to listen to the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: If we ever hear anything like that and we are called upon, we will go there and we will provide resources.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: We will provide a way to listen, but also to say, here's how we can support you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Work with my partner organizations and go to the communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Please, we'll meet.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I'd love to hear more from you and ideas that you have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: I will definitely make that happen.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam notes Councilmember Pierina Sanchez has joined, thanks the three administration witnesses for their informative testimony, and transitions to the public testimony portion of the hearing.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam closes administration panel and notes CM Sanchez arrival

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'd just like to note that we have been joined by Councilmember Pierina Sanchez as well.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'd like to thank the administration for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: It has been...

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Informative.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam announces rules for public testimony: three minutes per speaker, no video recording from the witness table, written testimony may be submitted to the sergeant at arms or via email within 72 hours. He calls the first panel of three witnesses.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam opens public testimony with decorum instructions

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I now open this hearing for public testimony, and I want to remind members of the public that this is a formal government proceeding and that decorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Furthermore, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but they may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant at Arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with the sergeant at arms and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: When you are recognized, you will have exactly three minutes to speak on today's hearing topic.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: If you have a written statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the sergeant at arms, and you may email written testimony to testimony at council.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: dot nyc dot gov which within 72 hours of this hearing audio and video recordings will not be accepted

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: For the first panel, we would like to call

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Leo Cesar Herrera, Scott Richman, and Animal.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'm going to spell the last name.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: It's A-U-L-A-K-H.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: You may begin in any order you like.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: You have...

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: You have exactly two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Sorry, three minutes.

chapters.summary: Scott Richman of ADL reports 2025 was the third highest year for antisemitic incidents since 1979, with NYC recording 1,160 incidents (74% within the five boroughs). He urges the Council to promote community-based education programs like ADL's No Place for Hate initiative and ADL Aura, an AI-powered education assistant for K-12 educators.
chapters.title: Scott Richman (ADL) testifies on antisemitic incident data and education programs

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: Chair Salam and members of the Committee to Combat Hate, thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: My name is Scott Richman, and I am honored to serve as the regional director for ADL's New York, New Jersey region.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: ADL is the leading anti-hate organization in the world dedicated since 1913 to fighting anti-Semitism and securing justice and fair treatment to all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: It is incumbent upon all of us to address and speak out on how we can collectively prevent and respond to hate in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: As an example of such hate, let me paint the picture when it comes to fighting hate against the Jewish community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: For nearly five decades, ADL has compiled an annual audit of anti-Semitic incidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: The audit is drawn from reports submitted by victims, community leaders, law enforcement partners, and media documentation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: ADL tracks, verifies, and in many cases responds to these incidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: 2025 was the third highest year for anti-Semitic incidents since ADL began tracking data in 1979, nearly double pre-October 7th record highs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: The reality is that Jewish Americans continue to be harassed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: assaulted and targeted an average of 17 times every day across this country.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: New York State is the epicenter of this crisis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: In 2025, New York recorded 1,160

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: anti-Semitic incidents more than any other state in the nation, accounting for nearly one in five of all incidents recorded across the United States.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: And 74% of those incidents occurred within the five boroughs of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: To respond to this surge in anti-Semitism and to all forms of hate and bias,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: ADL urges the council to promote community-based education programs that ensure the safety, inclusion, and support of the Jewish community and all communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: Programs like ADL's No Place for Hate initiative offers such tools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: No Place for Hate provides a framework for over 300 schools and over 200,000 students across New York and New Jersey to promote safe and inclusive school communities where every student can thrive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: The program is free.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: For the schools, dozens of which are currently using it in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: But we need help to get the word out about this effective means for combating hate and bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: We also urge the council to support educator training and resources that empower teachers to address anti-Semitism and all forms of hate with confidence and accuracy in their work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: To support this critical work, ADL has developed innovative tools like ADL Aura, our

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: artificial intelligence-powered education assistant that provides K-12 educators and community members with instant access to trusted, expert-vetted lesson plans and teaching strategies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: On Jewish identity, anti-Semitism, and the Holocaust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: ADL looks forward to continuing our partnership with this committee, the New York City Council, and with the communities it serves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: We are grateful for your attention to this issue and stand ready to assist with data, resources, programs, and recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

chapters.summary: Julio Cesar Herrera of Hispanic Federation describes HF's role as a PATH anchor organization since 2021, reaching millions of New Yorkers through sub-grantees providing anti-bias education across five boroughs. He warns that without sustained investment, many CBOs will be forced to scale back or suspend services.
chapters.title: Julio Cesar Herrera (Hispanic Federation) testifies on PATH initiative impact

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
