utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vijay Ramjatin
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: I'd like to extend my thanks to the chair, Yusef-Salaam, and for the Committee to Combat Hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: My name is Julio Cesar Herrera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: I serve as a government operations director for Hispanic Federation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: A nonprofit dedicated to empowering Latina communities through programs and advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Our testimony reflects the collective insights and experiences of HF and more than 100 of New York City community-based partners involved in the city's Partners Against the Hate Forward initiative.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: HF has proudly served as an anchor organization for PATH in partnership with the Mayor's Office of Prevention of Hate Crimes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Since 2021, this initiative has allowed us to fund, convene, and work alongside trusted CBOs that are helping New Yorkers understand, report, and prevent hate crimes and bias incidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Through PATH, Hispanic Federation and its partners have reached out to residents who are too often vulnerable to harassment, discrimination, and targeted violence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Our partners consist of CBOs who intimately engage their own communities through

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: culturally responsive, anti-bias, anti-discrimination, anti-hate education across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: To date, through this work, Hispanic Federation and our sub-grantees have reached millions of individuals across New York City, and that reach reflects thousands of moments where community members were able to learn their rights, ask questions,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: better understand available resources, and feel less alone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Despite the reach and measured deliverables of the PATH initiative, this endeavor remains deeply vulnerable without sustained investment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: If support is reduced or eliminated, many of our subgrantees carrying out this work will be forced to scale back programming, limit outreach, or suspend services altogether.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: For those organizations who already operate with limited resources,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Even a modest disruption in funding could have significant consequences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Hispanic Federation adamantly believes that combating hate is both a public safety responsibility and a statement of who we are as a city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: We are deeply grateful to the council for its continued leadership and for understanding how imperative PATH is to bolster the voices of the most marginalized members of our communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: We urge the council to continue supporting this critical initiative and the community-based organizations who are the bedrock of this initiative and on the front lines of preventing hate, supporting victims, and building a safer, more inclusive New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Thank you for this opportunity to testify and for your ongoing commitment.

chapters.summary: Amol Aulakh of the Sikh Coalition describes how Sikhs remain the third most targeted religious group per FBI data. He urges support for community-based reporting pathways, multilingual materials, anti-bias education accurately reflecting Sikh communities, and rapid response support for targeted gurdwaras, businesses, and families.
chapters.title: Amol Aulakh (Sikh Coalition) testifies on hate prevention investments

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Thank you as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: My name is Amol Alik, and I'm speaking on behalf of the Sikh Coalition in support of meaningful, sustained investments in hate crime and violence prevention in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: The Sikh Coalition was founded in the aftermath of 9-11, when Sikh Americans, especially those who wear turbans and maintain articles of faith, were targeted with violence, discrimination, and profiling.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: National FBI data continues to indicate Sikhs as the third most targeted religious group, with many incidents remaining unreported, misclassified, or misunderstood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: That is why the Sikh Coalition supports the proposed investments from the Office for the Prevention of Hate Crimes and the Office of Community Safety with these priorities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: It should include community-based and better reporting pathways so people who experience hate, harassment, or bias can seek support without being forced into unfamiliar systems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Especially for immigrants, workers, elders, and those with limited English proficiency.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: It should include additional funding for community organizations that have established trust in culturally competent ways, such as the Path Forward initiative.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Organizations need full-time capacity and multilingual materials to respond to what impacted communities are experiencing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: It should include proactive relationship building to fund and expand programs that promote mutual understanding in communities before tensions escalate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: It should include anti-bias prevention education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: accurately reflects the Sikh community, our articles of faith, and our history, where that lack of understanding can have real safety consequences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: It should include rapid response and survivor support, so when a Gurdwara Sahibji, small business, taxi driver, student, or families targeted, they can receive language-accessible resources,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: legal referrals, mental health support, and clear communication from city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: We believe that all New Yorkers deserve to live without the fear of being targeted because of who they are or how they are perceived.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: We urge this committee to utilize this budget increase to support these investments in partnership with communities that are impacted the most.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam thanks all three panelists and gives a special shout-out to Hispanic Federation for their partnership at a Bodega Festival event over the weekend, emphasizing the importance of bringing positive community events to neighborhoods.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam thanks first panel and highlights Hispanic Federation Bodega Festival partnership

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I just want to say thank you for your testimony to all three of you, but a special shout-out to Hispanic Federation for your partnership this weekend.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: There was a beautiful event that we held.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I think that's a big part of the work is just bringing the positive to our communities and bringing people together like that.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: So thank you for your partnership on the Bodega Festival.

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks about HF's experience reducing underreporting. Herrera describes a multi-pronged approach including CBO-led anti-bias education, bilingual PSA campaigns, and radio mentions. A 2023 survey of 10,000 New Yorkers found 31% didn't know what legally constituted a hate crime and 78% who had experienced one never reported it.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions Hispanic Federation on underreporting of hate crimes

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And also, I just have a question for the Hispanic Federation.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Your work to reduce on the reporting of hate crimes,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What has your experience been?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: In terms of, I'm sorry, if you could elaborate a little further.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So the work, specifically your work to reduce underreporting of the hate crimes that have been happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I just wanted to know what your experience has been.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: So there are two approaches to that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: The first is through our CBOs who intimately pack a lot of anti-bias work into a lot of the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: already ongoing curriculum that they employ in their communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Of course, even as an institution such as ours, everyone has blinders on, so we refer best to the organizations that are already based in the communities that we're looking to serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: And from HF's standpoint, we've also reached out on several outreach campaigns,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: bilingual PSA campaigns, both in English and Spanish, in key population junctions, as well as had several mentions of anti-bias, anti-discriminatory campaigns over the radio.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: And over, just to reference from 2023,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: So in 2023, when we deployed 10 community canvassers and surveyed more than 10,000 New Yorkers, we saw that 31% didn't know what legally constituted a hate crime.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: And then after identifying which 78% already experienced that, they had experienced one and never reported it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: So with that, a lot of...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: I'm just trying to get my words together because it's a multi, so it's a multi-pronged approach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: With that, we've seen that in the communities and the different populations that we affect, be that from different sexuality, race, creed,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: a lot of them start to realize the fact that they had already experienced a hate crime, that they never really understood because of the banality of it,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: how profound and how pervasive it can be on a day-to-day interaction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: So that was something that, from our experience, is something that's far too prevalent, if that better answers your question.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It definitely does, and thank you for describing it, because I know oftentimes those have been close to the pain.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: You know, if they have a seat at the table, they can, you know, really, really do the best work, especially when it comes to fixing this issue.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Just have one more.

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks all three panelists about challenges working across language communities. Herrera describes relying on diverse coalition partners like La Voz de Unidas and NICE. Aulakh emphasizes bilingual materials for Punjabi-speaking gurdwara communities. Richman discusses hiring Orthodox community members who speak Yiddish to build trust and improve reporting.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions first panel on language community challenges

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And this is a question for all three of you about your work with different language communities.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What are the challenges that you may have been encountering in working across different language communities?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: I think just going back to CBO outreach, of course, we have over 100 partners spread out across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: A lot of that includes in areas in Queens that might not be our specific, let's say, demographic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: For example, La Voz de Unidas, that was referenced before, they not only employ and identify safe spaces for LGBTQIA folks,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: other groups such as New Immigrant Community Empowerment focuses on training undocumented day laborers based on workplace bias, discrimination, as

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: packed into already existing know your rights training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: And that cuts across different languages, different ethnicities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: So something that we rely heavily on the coalition of partners that we have that cut across different sectors of identities and better represent the mosaic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: Of this city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: From the work that I do as well, given that we work with the Sikh community, our community really needs bilingual materials and resources to effectively communicate their needs and to help understand what's going on within our community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: And so we heavily work with, you know, Gurdwara Saibjis, which are Sikh houses of worship, who predominantly also speak in Punjabi as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: So we do our best to provide Know Your Rights resources as well, you know, provide presentations and make sure that they have all the information that they need to know how to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: access resources, legal referrals, mental health support, and various other components as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: So we really take pride in the fact that we do our best to make sure that our community's needs are being heard while, you know, using our own capacity to translate their needs effectively as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: I would just add, in terms of our community, the Jewish community, the language that is a little bit more difficult to manage is Yiddish.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: There is a significant Yiddish-speaking population here in New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: Many of them speak English, but not all, or not so well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: And reaching them in that language is important.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: And I can actually relate to the other question that you asked of my colleague here, that the issue of underreporting, a major way to combat underreporting, is...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: to build those relationships.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: So what we've done is we actually hired two people who are members of the Orthodox community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: That was relatively new for us a few years ago, and it has been very, very important, very important both for reaching them because they are Yiddish speakers,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: but also for making sure that there's a trust that's developed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: And when there's a trust that's developed, people report.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: So we were receiving very few reports from the Orthodox community just a few years ago, and that has changed tremendously, and it's changed tremendously because of that trust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: And the language barrier is broken, you know, very, very, very important to have that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: And I will just add that Orthodox Jews in the city are disproportionately affected by such incidents because they are what we call visibly identifiable Jews.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: So very, very important.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Scott Richman
utterances.text: And that's why the organization made the commitment to hire people that could build that kind of trust and have those language skills.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

chapters.summary: Hilary Stuchin of UJA Federation describes supporting nearly 100 nonprofit partners serving New Yorkers of all backgrounds. She urges the city to invest the proposed $29M for OPHC in effective programming, improve reporting systems in multiple languages, and invest in educator preparedness as an essential prevention strategy.
chapters.title: Hilary Stuchin (UJA Federation) testifies on antisemitism and CBO partnerships

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'd now like to call the second panel.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Which consists of Sophia Tobia Gage and Hilary Stutchen.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And you each have three minutes and may begin in whichever order you'd like.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: Hi there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: Chair Salam, members of the committee, thank you so much for hosting this hearing today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: I'm Hilary Stuchin, and I'm here on behalf of UJA Federation of New York, and thank you so much for providing the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: UJA supports nearly 100 nonprofit partners that serve New Yorkers of all backgrounds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: We serve older adults, children, families, immigrants, survivors of trauma, people with disabilities, individuals facing poverty and food insecurity, as well as isolation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: We also work to strengthen Jewish life, combat anti-Semitism, and build bridges across communities throughout the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: We appreciate the council's continued attention to the alarming rise in hate and bias incidents in the city, which harm more than just the targeted individual.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: These events send a message to communities that they may not be safe in their neighborhoods, schools, houses of worship, workplaces, or public spaces.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: For Jewish New Yorkers in particular, this fear is not abstract.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: Anti-Semitic incidents, as you've heard, continue to represent a disproportionate share of reported hate crimes in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: And Jewish institutions, on their end, have operated at heightened vigilance since October 2023.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: So CBOs in this are essential partners in both the prevention, education, and response to hate and bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: They're trusted by their communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: They serve and can help people navigate trauma, access mental health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: report social services, and understand the reporting options that they have to build a sense of safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: We ask the council as well as the city to prioritize the following steps and actions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: We appreciate the proposed $29 million for the Office of Prevention of Hate Crimes, and we urge the administration to invest those resources in effective programming that addresses the underlying causes of hate and bias, including anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: The city should continue to improve reporting systems and outreach so New Yorkers know the difference between what a hate crime is, a bias incident, discrimination complaints, and emergencies, and know where to go for help in their preferred language.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: We also believe that the city should continue to invest in education and music prevention where schools and youth-serving programs are critical settings for addressing bias before it escalates to harm.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: It's similarly important to make sure that teachers are aware and informed so they can recognize these incidents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: And we encourage the city to view educator preparedness as an essential component of this prevention strategy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: CBO's community-based prevention should include partnerships with organizations that equip teachers and schools with the resources and support necessary to foster understanding our differences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: And I see I have 23 seconds left, so I'll just say thank you for the opportunity to testify, and I will submit written testimony separately.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Thank you as well.

chapters.summary: Sophia Tobia Gage of CAIR NY reports a 130% national rise in anti-Muslim hate crimes between 2020-2025. CAIR's data shows 64% of Muslims experienced hate crimes or bias incidents, yet only 4% reported to law enforcement. She notes NYPD recorded only 43 anti-Muslim hate crimes in 2024 while CAIR received significantly more reports.
chapters.title: Sophia Tobia Gage (CAIR NY) testifies on anti-Muslim hate crime data gaps

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: The Council on American Islamic Relations New York would like to thank Chair Yusef-Salaam and the entire committee to combat hate for the opportunity to provide testimony here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: My name is Sophia Tobia Gage and I'm speaking on behalf of CARE New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: CARE New York is the largest Muslim civil rights and advocacy organization in New York State.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Our mission is to protect the civil liberties, promote justice, and empower Muslim New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: We serve the 800,000 to 1 million Muslim New Yorkers from extremely diverse backgrounds, racial and ethnic origins, as well as non-Muslim communities that have long been among the primary targets of hate crimes in this city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: As many of you know, Mayor Mamdani invested $26 million into the Department of Community Safety, which includes the Office for the Prevention of Hate Crimes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: This investment is a meaningful step toward addressing the persistent and deeply harmful patterns of hate and violence our communities have faced, ensuring this city flourishes as a place for all who live here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: We urge the City Council to match the Mayor's commitment and to ensure these resources reach the community-based organizations like Care New York that are on the front lines of this work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Since October 2023, Care New York witnessed a dramatic surge in reports of bias related to incidents in the subway, in schools, workplaces, and neighborhoods where community is meant to gather and feel safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: These trends reveal that we are not dealing with isolated incidents of discrimination, of violence, or of harassment, but rather this is a true reflection of the broader climate of dehumanization.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: of vulnerable communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: This requires urgency and coordination between the city and said community organizations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: The numbers speak for themselves, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Based on figures Care New York has carefully collected, hate crimes against Muslims has risen an alarming 130% between 2020 and 2025 nationally.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: This represents a small fraction of actual incidents as our communities consistently under report due to fear and distrust and a lack of accessible resources.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: In our...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: In our Feeling the Hate report, we found that 64% of Muslims have experienced a hate crime, bias incident, or both.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Yet only 4% of those who experienced a hate crime reported it to law enforcement because they either did not trust that or did not feel like a productive action to report to such agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: And lastly, that 75% of Muslim women have experienced a hate crime, a bias incident, or both.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: In 2024, the NYPD recorded only 43 anti-Muslim hate crimes in New York City, yet as I demonstrated, Care New York received significantly more reports from our community that same year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: We are capturing more incidents than the official police data reflects, which means there is a persistent and troubling gap between official data and the reality on the ground.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: On a statewide level, the New York State Comptroller reports that hate crimes against Muslim New Yorkers rose 106% between 2018 and 2018.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: These are not abstract data points.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: These are people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: These are our entire communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Community-based organizations have been leading hate-based hate violence prevention efforts for years, fighting identity-based violence and trauma-informed support to hate crime survivors long before dedicated city funding existed for this purpose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Care New York is a direct...

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks CAIR to clarify the 130% increase timeframe and whether there is NYC-specific data. Gage confirms the figure covers 2020-2025 nationally and that CAIR's state-level data reflects the gap between official NYPD numbers and actual community reports, with detailed case files available for follow-up.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions CAIR on national vs. NYC hate crime data

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I do have a question.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Well, the time expired, but I do have a question.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: You said something about, and I just wanted to get the year right, up 130% nationally between which years was that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Between the years 2020 and 2025.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Any drill down for New York City itself?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Say it one more time.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Any data that specifically deals towards New York City itself?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I know that's nationally, but.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Yes, so Care New York represents not just New York City, but also New York State, so the data that we reflected in our Feeling the Heat report reflects the entire state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: But in that report, sorry, give me one second.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: We have seen, yeah, I think so, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: I don't have the data specifically on the amount of reports, but that can be reflected in our larger, in our requests for assistance and case files and documents.

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks both panelists about language barrier experiences. Stuchin highlights Yiddish-speaking and Russian-speaking communities in Brooklyn needing resources in their languages. Gage notes CAIR's bilingual Arabic-English staff and use of partner translators when needed.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions second panel on language barriers and underreporting

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Yeah, the previous panel we discussed and asked them about also the underreporting based on language barriers and things of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So I really want to ask that same question to both of you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Has there been any experience that you all have had as it relates to language barriers, underreporting?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I know Kara mentioned the data that they've collected that's not

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Trying to find a better word than equal to what the NYPD has been collecting.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: But really, I just wanted to know from both of you, and we can start with you, what has the experience been in terms of the language barriers and underreporting and things of that nature?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: No, so we work with a citywide group of over 100 nonprofits or close to 100 nonprofits dealing with all sorts of communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: But I will say, in addition to the Yiddish-speaking community, which is prominent within...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: Brooklyn and elsewhere.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: Also in South Brooklyn, the Russian-speaking community often needs resources in order to access various supports and understand where they can go should a bias incident or a hate incident occur.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: At CARE New York, many of the members of our education staff are bilingual and so are speaking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: The speaking Arabic, which is the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Arabic and English are the main languages that our constituents are speaking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: And then also, additionally, with the partner organizations that we work with, there are often translators if need be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: But that hasn't been an issue necessarily.

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks whether any data exists on hate against Black Jewish communities and people from the African diaspora in his Harlem district. Both panelists offer to follow up with specific data, with Gage noting African and African American Muslim communities are a large portion of CAIR's constituents.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions panel on Black Jewish and African diaspora community data

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm curious to know as well,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm trying to figure out how to state this.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What I'm curious to know is, has any of your data surrounded, for the Jewish community, the

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: black Jewish community and any hate reporting, because in my district, in District 9 in Harlem, we do have a Jewish community that is black.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And in addition to care, anything

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: dealing with like the, I mean, we have like a whole little Africa community that involves all kinds of folks from the African diaspora.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I'm just wondering, has there been any information as it relates to people of,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I don't want to just say African-American descent because they've been categorized like that if they don't have their yarmulke on or kufi on or something like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: Yeah, no, so I personally don't have that information available right now, but we're more than happy to follow up with your office and provide answers to any of these questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: I will say this, you know, we understand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: That we go back to the underreporting of it all, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: So, and what data is available to look back to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Hilary Stuchin
utterances.text: So I'll happily get that information for you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sophia Tobia Gage
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm also happy to get that information and send it at a later time, but I will say that the African and African-American Muslim communities are a large majority of our constituents and groups that we, yeah, are very important that we work with often.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Madison Mountie of the Simon Wiesenthal Center describes two initiatives: Combating Hate in Digital Spaces workshops for grades 7-12 covering digital identity, AI, and algorithm manipulation, and Mobile Museums of Tolerance bringing immersive human rights education directly to schools and communities across NYC.
chapters.title: Madison Mountie (Simon Wiesenthal Center) testifies on digital literacy education

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And now we have the third panel.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Madison Mountie and Marissa Barak.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And we also have on Zoom Leslie Claire Allen.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: You may begin in any order you like.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: You each have three minutes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Salam and members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Thank you for convening this critical hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: My name is Madison Mountie, and I am the Director of Government Relations and Advocacy for the Simon Wiesenthal Center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: For decades, the SWC has fought anti-Semitism, hate, and bias by focusing on the most powerful tool for long-term prevention, education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: We are at a defining moment in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: The data shows a sobering, persistent rise in identity-based harassment and hate crimes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: While reactive measures are necessary, true safety begins with proactive community prevention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: We must reach people before bias takes root, especially our youth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: I'm here to highlight the important work that we are doing locally across the five boroughs through two vital community-based initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: First are Combating Hate in Digital Spaces workshops.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: In longstanding partnership with the City Council, SWC and our team of experienced educators have reached thousands of New York City public school students through our educational workshops.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Combating Hate in Digital Spaces

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: is a learning experience designed for grades 7 through 12 and offered in classrooms across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: With a focus on digital identity, AI, and civic responsibility, these workshops provide students with an identity Venn diagram that supports reflection on how their digital selves are shaped,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: as well as the responsibilities and real-world consequences that accompany their online presence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Learners explore the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: formation of online identities and examine how the algorithm influences and manipulates individual actions in digital spaces.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Second, our mobile museums of tolerance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: We know that access to high quality immersive education shouldn't depend on a school's budget or zip code.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Our MMOTs are a state of the art traveling classroom that brings world class human rights and anti-bias education directly to New York City neighborhoods through the support of New York State.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Our two MMOTs bring immersive tolerance education directly to schools and communities across New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: We use cutting-edge digital storytelling to engage audiences in learning about the Holocaust, civil rights movement, digital literacy, anti-Semitism, racism, bullying, and social justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: These programs are not just concepts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: They are operational blueprints for community-based prevention that build empathy and resilience directly inside our neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: To scale these efforts and meet the current and growing need, we cannot do it alone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: We urge the City Council to prioritize sustained investments in mobile accessible education and SWC's digital literacy workshops so we can reach every public school and community center in this city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Safety and dignity cannot be selective.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: We must protect every single New Yorker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: We applaud this committee's ongoing leadership and stand ready to partner with you to expand these vital educational resources.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Amol Alik
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Marissa Sabarak of AJC New York provides historical context that the term 'antisemitism' was invented in 1879 specifically for anti-Jewish hatred. She cites AJC data showing 56% of Jewish New Yorkers changed their behavior out of fear, and urges focus on the AJC's Combating Antisemitism Playbook priorities including education and intergovernmental coordination.
chapters.title: Marissa Sabarak (AJC New York) testifies on antisemitism definition and prevention

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Salam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: My name is Marissa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Sabarak, and I'm the Assistant Director of AJC New York, the New York City Regional Office of the American Jewish Committee, the global advocacy organization for the Jewish people since 1906.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Before I begin, I want to provide a historical point regarding the definition of anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: The term anti-Semitism was invented in 1879 specifically as a term for anti-Jewish hatred by a racist German pseudoscientist to legitimize hatred against Jews.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Using it to cover other groups can accidentally erase the unique history of anti-Jewish bigotry and make it harder to precisely address.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: According to AJC's 2025 State of Anti-Semitism in America report, 56% of Jewish New Yorkers reported changing their behavior out of fear of anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: More than half of Jewish New Yorkers aren't merely worried about anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: They're changing how they live because of it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: That's what fear looks like in practice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: And if more than half of Jewish New Yorkers are changing their behavior because they're afraid, we should be asking whether the city's current approach to preventing hate and bias is adequately protecting a community that remains the target of more than half of all reported hate crimes in our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: If the city is serious about prevention, it has to acknowledge that anti-Semitism isn't fueled only by extremists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: It's also fueled when public figures normalize rhetoric that casts Jews, Jewish institutions, or core aspects of Jewish identity as suspect, unwelcome, or you

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: uniquely deserving of scrutiny.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: As the Council evaluates the City's approach to hate and bias, AJC encourages a focus on the priorities outlined in our Combating Anti-Semitism Playbook,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: including strengthening relationships between government and communities, improving education about contemporary manifestations of anti-Semitism,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: increasing coordination across institutions, and ensuring that policymakers have the information they need to respond effectively.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your time and your consideration.

chapters.summary: CM Krishnan asks SWC about their experience combating online hate. Mountie describes updating workshops to cover algorithm influence, helping students recognize hate speech and bias online, and teaching them how their interactions perpetuate harmful content. SWC plans to expand programming to elementary and college levels.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions SWC on combating hate in digital spaces

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you both for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I do have a question for SWC.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It feels like the Internet and social media continue to grow as the epicenter.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Of so much hate.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Can you talk a bit more about SWC's experience in combating discrimination in digital spaces and how that work may be growing, if it is?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Yeah, so something that our education team is constantly evaluating is updating our digital literacy workshops and meeting students where they are and meeting the moment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: So something new for this year that we incorporated is background on the algorithm.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: And what we find is a lot of students walk away from our workshops having a better understanding of what is hate speech and bias online.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: They don't recognize a lot of these things initially, and that's something that we help them educate themselves on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: And we also help them learn how what they interact with online can then further perpetuate what they see.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Julio Cesar Herrera
utterances.text: around messaging.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: disinformation, hate speech, bias, and how sometimes what they interact with is constituted as bullying or a hate crime.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: And so I think we start as young as we can, and we're looking to start even younger than seventh grade, as early as third grade, elementary school, and we're also looking to expand into college campuses so that we can really

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: reach students from the beginning and give them a better informed sense of an ethical online presence and really help promote tolerance and mutual respect over division and hate speech.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam, noting his 10 children, asks about experiences reaching younger children exposed to hate on digital platforms like YouTube. Mountie describes developing age-appropriate curriculum for third graders. Sabarak emphasizes the need for honest parent-child conversations and holding social media companies accountable for platform hate.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions both panelists on reaching younger children online

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: One of the things that I've been seeing as well, I mean, I have 10 children, myself, the youngest is 10,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: is that oftentimes as parents or as educators and loved ones, we may be, and this is really for both of you, we may be engaged in activities like cooking or cleaning or something of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And oftentimes our children might be on those digital spaces and digital, you know, doing things like on YouTube or whatever.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And I'm just wondering what that experience has been because, of course, we know that seventh grade is really great, but we've been seeing trends that are going even further down.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Meaning attacking younger and younger individuals in order to try to make them into a group of people that actually hate, you know, because hate is taught.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And so I'm just wondering what...

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: both of your experiences have been as it relates to really pulling in this conversation towards younger individuals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Yeah, we're definitely seeing a greater need, like you said, for starting even younger than we currently do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: And it's something that, again, our education team is working on developing because we have to modify the workshops that we provide based on the age group.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: What works for a seventh grader or a fifth grader is not necessarily going to work for a second or third grader.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: So we have to make the curriculum and the topics that we're discussing equally as impactful and effective, but also understandable for the age that they're at.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: And part of that is learning and adapting how we deliver that through digital literacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: You know, we're not trying to promote increased usage of social media platforms or increase their time online even more than it already is because that's already growing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: But it's how to include what platforms they're already on or what they're being introduced to and making that the forefront and helping them recognize,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: again, what's hate speech online, helping them be upstanders rather than bystanders in those efforts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Thank you for your question, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: It's of course important for parents to have honest conversations with their children about different manifestations of hate and speaking specifically about hatred against Jews, about anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: It's such a nuanced form of hate because of its history.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: And there are different manifestations of anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Understanding Jewish peoplehood and Jewish history, Jewish American history, is of course an important part of being able to understand and then counter and prevent anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Another aspect, however, is also the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: the social media space as you spoke of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Parents, I'm sure, would love to be able to prohibit as much of their children's time on social media as possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: That's not always the reality, and we need to be able to hold social media and tech companies accountable for the hate that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: permeates across their platforms.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam asks what more the panelists want to see from intergovernmental coordination. Sabarak urges more coordination on prevention, policy, and education between the mayoral administration and City Council. Mountie highlights SWC's partnerships through the City Council for classroom programs and through the state for mobile museums.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam questions panelists on intergovernmental coordination for hate prevention

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Regarding intergovernmental coordination, do you have anything that you may want to elaborate regarding that space?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Regarding intergovernmental coordination.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Yeah, in terms of working with them, what would you like to see more of, preventing hate and things of that nature?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: Certainly there should be more coordination amongst the different government agencies and institutions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: We specifically would like to see them working more closely together on prevention and things like policy and education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: I think that is a core piece that is important for a city like New York that has a mayoral administration and a co-equal branch that is the city council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: And for me, our programs, our classroom programs are supported longstanding partnership through the city council and then our mobile museums of tolerance are supported through the state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: And as it relates to our classroom programming, we'd love to be able to partner with more council districts and bring our programs to as many public schools across the five boroughs as we possibly can.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Mountie
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Leslie Claire Allen of the NYC Anti-Violence Project, a PATH partner, testifies remotely about AVP's 24/7 crisis hotline for survivors of hate violence. She reports hotline calls up 35% and an extensive legal services waitlist, urging investment in organizations like AVP to meet rising needs amid national anti-LGBTQ sentiment, especially against trans communities and LGBTQ immigrants.
chapters.title: Leslie Claire Allen (AVP) testifies on LGBTQ hate violence and survivor support

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And on Zoom, if you are available, Leslie Claire Allen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: But that's also different because some of them are part of the group.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Sabarak
utterances.text: You could begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: Can everyone hear me?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: First, I want to apologize for not being there in person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: As you can hear from my voice, I'm very sick and I didn't want to get anyone else sick.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: But my name is Leslie Allen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: I use pronouns she, hers, and I'm the deputy, I'm the director of legal services at the New York City Anti-Violence Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: The New York City Anti-Violence Project is one of the PATH chairs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: I'm so sorry, I'm really struggling today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: We have been proudly working with PATH for the past five years, and I believe that our work is extremely meaningful in helping prevent LGBTQ.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: Communities from facing extreme violence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: But in part of both the prevention of hate violence, there has to also be the structure there to help survivors of violence who

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: The hate has not been stopped and they've actually been harmed in the present.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: And AVP very proudly has a 24-hour, seven-day-a-week crisis hotline where survivors can call who may be

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: uninterested in calling the police, maybe attempted to call the police and didn't get the response that they wanted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: Or simply don't really even understand how to place context into whatever hate violence they experience and don't know what they want to do next.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: AVP's long history of working with city council to stop violence towards LGBTQ people is something that we're extremely proud of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: We believe that the new investment from the Windami administration, what we hope will be seen from the city council,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: will usher in a new bright era for New York City that really allows us to be a beacon of hope while nationally we're seeing unprecedented rise

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: of anti-LGBTQ sentiment, especially towards the transgender communities and towards LGBTQ immigrants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: Our hotline calls are up 35%, which is very significant because we already thought of our hotline as sort of reaching maximum volume.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: It's becoming harder and harder to meet the needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: We have an extensive wait list for legal services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: We really need to see this future investment into more organizations like AVP in order to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: help meet the people, meet the people's needs who are experiencing hate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: And I'm so sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Claire Allen
utterances.text: I'm going to let you go because I'm having such trouble talking, but thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony and hopefully you'll have a speedy recovery.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And if you need to reach out to us to provide written testimony, that definitely is available to you at testimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Salaam calls a final Zoom panel of three individuals who do not appear. He offers a last call for in-person testimony, and seeing none, concludes the hearing and thanks all who testified.
chapters.title: Chair Salaam calls final Zoom panel, no one appears, closes hearing

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'd now like to call the last panel via Zoom, Glyn Balofsky, Jennifer Nicole Almeida, and Christopher Leon Johnson.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Starting time.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Okay, I don't see them on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: We will do a last call in the room.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: If anyone else wishes to testify, you may testify by filling out an appearance form with the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Seeing none, that concludes today's business.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

summary: Committee to Combat Hate holds oversight hearing on community-based prevention and response to hate and bias, with testimony from OPHC, MOCA, and OFCP directors

OPHC Director Ramjatin highlights 800% funding increase to $29M and programs including PATH, 7 on 7 community initiative, and Youth Ambassador Leadership Program; Chair Salaam debates MOCA Director on definition of antisemitism
Public testimony from ADL, Hispanic Federation, Sikh Coalition, UJA Federation, CAIR NY, Simon Wiesenthal Center, AJC, and AVP urges sustained investment in community-based hate prevention
title: Committee to Combat Hate
